title: Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pump – Occlusion Alarm / Will Not Start Infusion
description: Structured Clinical Engineering approach for resolving occlusion alarms on a Baxter SIGMA Spectrum infusion pump before assuming internal failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the pump is attached to an active patient, coordinate with clinical staff to transition the patient to another infusion pump before troubleshooting. Do not troubleshoot extensively while the device is actively infusing a critical medication.

steps.title: Inspect the IV Set and Tubing Path
steps.instructions: Verify the tubing is correctly loaded into the pump channel. Confirm there are no kinks, sharp bends, compression points, or twisted tubing from the pump to the patient. Check that all roller clamps and slide clamps are fully open. Even minor tubing restriction can generate an occlusion alarm.

steps.title: Check the Patient-Side of the Line
steps.instructions: Inspect the distal tubing and catheter site (visually only — do not manipulate invasive access unless authorized). Look for obvious downstream obstruction such as: IV pole hooks compressing tubing. Arm positioning causing line occlusion. Secondary tubing backflow issues. Occlusion alarms often originate downstream of the pump.

steps.title: Inspect the Cassette and Pump Door
steps.instructions: Open the pump door and visually inspect the cassette seating. Ensure the disposable set is fully aligned and seated properly in the channel. Close the door firmly and verify it latches completely. An improperly seated cassette can cause false occlusion detection.

steps.title: Confirm Fluid Source and Prime Status
steps.instructions: Verify the fluid bag contains adequate volume. Ensure the line has been properly primed and contains no large air gaps. Check that the spike is fully inserted and that the vent (if applicable) is correctly positioned.

steps.title: Review Alarm Details on Display
steps.instructions: Note the exact alarm wording (e.g., “Upstream Occlusion” vs. “Downstream Occlusion” if displayed). Observe whether the alarm triggers immediately upon start or after pressure builds. Documenting the exact alarm behavior is important for CCR documentation.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Reset
steps.instructions: Stop the infusion. Remove and reload the tubing. Close the door securely. Restart the infusion and observe pressure behavior. If the alarm clears and infusion resumes normally, monitor briefly to ensure stable operation.

steps.title: If the Problem Persists
steps.instructions: If the occlusion alarm continues after completing all external checks, common causes have been ruled out. Remove the pump from clinical service. Clearly label it “Out of Service”. Send for bench evaluation or repair. Persistent occlusion alarms after tubing and cassette verification may indicate internal pressure sensor or mechanism issues. Knowing when to stop bedside troubleshooting is proper Clinical Engineering practice.

CCR.Complaint: “Pump will not start infusion. Occlusion alarm displayed.”
CCR.Cause: “Tubing inspected and free of kinks. Clamps open. Cassette reseated. Fluid present and line primed. Alarm persists after reload. Suspect internal pressure sensing issue.”
CCR.Resolution: “Removed pump from service, labeled Out of Service, sent to CE shop for evaluation.” or “Bench tested; unable to clear occlusion alarm. Forwarded for repair.”

helpfulDetails: Any abnormal sounds or resistance felt

title: Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pump – Keypad / Screen Not Responding
description: Troubleshooting guide for unresponsive keypad or frozen screen on a Baxter SIGMA Spectrum infusion pump, focusing on external causes before internal hardware failure.

steps.title: Ensure the Pump Is Not Actively Infusing a Patient
steps.instructions: Before troubleshooting, confirm the pump is not actively running on a patient. If it is, transition the patient to another pump before proceeding. This protects patient safety and allows proper evaluation.

steps.title: Check for Keypad Lock or Software Lock State
steps.instructions: Look for any “Keypad Locked” or similar icon on the display. Attempt the standard key combination used to unlock the keypad (per facility policy). Verify the pump is not in a mode restricting input (e.g., alarm screen requiring acknowledgment). A locked keypad can appear as a failure when it is functioning normally.

steps.title: Perform a Soft Reset
steps.instructions: Place the pump in Standby if possible. Turn the pump OFF using the power button. Wait 10–15 seconds. Power it back ON. If the pump is frozen due to a software glitch, a power cycle often restores function. Observe: Does the screen respond during boot? Do keys respond during startup prompts? If responsiveness returns after reboot, the issue was likely software-related.

steps.title: Verify AC Power and Battery Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm the pump is connected to a known-good outlet. Check that the AC indicator is illuminated. Remove from AC power briefly and verify it operates on battery. Look for battery error messages or low-voltage behavior. Low or unstable power can cause display lag, freezing, or unresponsive buttons. If the screen flickers or reboots repeatedly, suspect battery failure.

steps.title: Inspect for Physical Obstruction or Contamination
steps.instructions: Check for dried IV fluids, cleaning residue, or debris around keys. Inspect the keypad membrane for: Cracks. Warping. Sticky or collapsed buttons. Look for fluid intrusion around screen edges. Contamination or membrane damage can physically prevent key actuation. Do not attempt internal cleaning. Surface cleaning only per facility protocol.

steps.title: Evaluate Screen vs. Keypad Function
steps.instructions: Determine if the issue is: Entire interface unresponsive. Only certain keys not responding. Display frozen but keypad still generating tones. Display blank but pump powered on. If: Audible key tones occur but screen does not change → display or software issue. No tones and no response → keypad or main board issue. Document these observations clearly.

steps.title: Check for Alarm or System Lock State
steps.instructions: If the pump is in alarm: Attempt to silence/acknowledge the alarm. Verify the screen is not stuck on a critical fault requiring service mode. Some faults prevent further input until addressed.

steps.title: Attempt Operation in Biomed Test Mode (If Authorized)
steps.instructions: If permitted by facility policy: Enter basic self-test or diagnostics mode. Observe if keypad input registers in test screens. If keypad fails in diagnostic mode, hardware failure is likely. Do not perform internal repairs.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported keypad and screen were not responding. Unable to change infusion rate.
CCR.Cause: Pump powered on but keypad produced no audible tones. Screen frozen during infusion menu. Issue persisted after power cycle. No external contamination observed. Unit failed keypad response in diagnostic mode.
CCR.Resolution: Removed pump from service. Labeled Out of Service. Sent to vendor for repair due to suspected internal keypad/controller failure.

helpfulDetails: Alarm condition at time of failure

title: Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pump – Battery Won’t Hold Charge / Won’t Power On
description: Troubleshooting guide for SIGMA Spectrum infusion pumps that will not power on using battery power or fail to hold a charge, focusing on external power verification before internal repair.

steps.title: Confirm Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the pump is connected to a patient and loses power, immediately transfer the infusion to another functioning pump. Do not troubleshoot while actively infusing unless patient safety is assured. Power issues can directly interrupt therapy. Always secure the patient first.

steps.title: Verify AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Confirm the outlet is live (test with outlet tester or plug in known-working device). Ensure the hospital-grade power cord is fully seated in both the wall outlet and pump. Inspect the cord for cuts or fraying, loose IEC connection, and bent blades. A loose AC connection is one of the most common causes of “dead” pumps.

steps.title: Check for Charging Indicators
steps.instructions: With AC connected, confirm the pump powers on. Observe battery charging icon on display. Check for any battery-related alerts or warnings. If the pump powers on with AC but immediately shuts off when unplugged, the internal battery is likely not holding a charge.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Power Test
steps.instructions: With the pump powered on and AC connected, allow the pump to charge for at least 30–60 minutes. Disconnect AC power and observe whether the pump remains powered, immediately shuts down, or displays a low battery warning. If it shuts down immediately after charging, suspect battery degradation.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Installation (If Accessible Without Disassembly)
steps.instructions: If your facility policy allows battery access, remove the pump from service. Inspect battery compartment for proper seating, corrosion, loose connectors, and swelling. Do not open internal electronics beyond standard battery access. If swelling or leakage is observed, remove from service immediately.

steps.title: Check for Excessive Heat or Odor
steps.instructions: During charging, feel for abnormal heat near battery compartment and note any unusual smell. Excess heat may indicate internal charging circuit issues.

steps.title: Review Service History
steps.instructions: Check maintenance records for age of battery, previous battery replacements, and repeated battery-related complaints. SIGMA Spectrum batteries have a finite lifespan and commonly require periodic replacement based on usage cycles.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reports pump shuts off when unplugged and will not stay on battery.
CCR.Cause: Pump powered normally on AC. After 45-minute charge, unit shut down immediately when AC removed. Battery failed functional hold test.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service. Replaced internal battery per facility protocol. Performed operational verification and battery runtime test. Returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (Returned to Service or Sent for Repair)

title: Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pump – Air-in-Line Alarms
description: Troubleshooting repeated or unexpected Air-in-Line alarms on a Baxter SIGMA Spectrum infusion pump using an external-first, patient-safety-focused approach.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot on an actively infusing critical patient without backup support. Transition the patient to another pump if clinically necessary.

steps.title: Confirm the Alarm Message
steps.instructions: Verify the exact alarm displayed (Air-in-Line vs. Occlusion or Cassette error). Document the specific wording for the work order.

steps.title: Inspect the IV Bag and Tubing for Visible Air
steps.instructions: Check the drip chamber for proper fill level (typically half full). Inspect the entire length of tubing for visible bubbles or large air pockets. If air is present, re-prime the set per hospital protocol. Why this matters: True air in the line is the most common and correctable cause.

steps.title: Check Cassette Seating and Door Closure
steps.instructions: Open the pump door. Remove and reseat the cassette. Ensure it is fully seated and the door closes completely without resistance. Listen for the latch to engage. Improper seating can misalign the ultrasonic air sensor.

steps.title: Inspect the Cassette and Tubing Channel
steps.instructions: Look for warped plastic, cracks, or deformed cassette housing. Ensure tubing is properly aligned in the pumping channel. Confirm the correct set type is being used (approved Baxter set). Off-brand or incorrect sets may trigger nuisance alarms.

steps.title: Clean the Air Sensor Area (External Only)
steps.instructions: With the cassette removed, inspect the air sensor window inside the pump door. Clean gently with approved wipes if there is dried fluid, residue, or debris. Allow the area to dry fully before reinstalling the set. Dried fluid or residue can interfere with ultrasonic detection.

steps.title: Test with a New IV Set
steps.instructions: Install a brand-new, properly primed Baxter set. Run a test infusion using saline. Observe if the alarm recurs. If the issue resolves with a new set, the original tubing or cassette was likely defective.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Mechanical Factors
steps.instructions: Ensure the pump is not being heavily jostled or mounted loosely. Confirm it is not positioned at extreme angles. Verify there are no kinks creating intermittent negative pressure.

steps.title: Perform a Functional Test (Off Patient)
steps.instructions: Run the pump at a moderate rate using saline. Observe for repeated alarms. Note whether alarms occur immediately or intermittently.

CCR.Complaint: Nurse reported repeated Air-in-Line alarms during normal infusion. Pump would not continue infusion.
CCR.Cause: Air bubbles found throughout tubing due to incomplete priming. Cassette reseated and tubing replaced.
CCR.Resolution: Re-primed line and replaced IV set. Pump tested with saline infusion; no further alarms observed.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (Returned to service / Sent for repair)

title: Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pump - Cassette / Set Not Recognized
description: Guide for troubleshooting when a SIGMA Spectrum infusion pump fails to recognize the cassette or IV set.

steps.title: Verify Pump Power and Status
steps.instructions: Ensure the pump is powered on and the display shows the main screen. Confirm battery or AC power is adequate; low power can prevent sensor operation. Why: Some pumps disable sensor detection under low battery or power instability.

steps.title: Check Cassette Orientation and Seating
steps.instructions: Remove the cassette/set and inspect the orientation. Ensure it is inserted correctly per the labeled guide. Re-seat the cassette firmly until it clicks into place. Why: Misalignment is a common cause of recognition failure.

steps.title: Inspect the Cassette/Sensor Interface
steps.instructions: Examine the pump’s sensor area and cassette for dirt, fluid, or debris. Wipe gently with a lint-free cloth if necessary. Why: Obstructions can block optical or mechanical sensors from detecting the cassette.

steps.title: Inspect the Cassette/Set Itself
steps.instructions: Check for visible damage, cracks, or expired tubing. Ensure the cassette is compatible with the SIGMA Spectrum pump model. Why: Non-compatible or damaged cassettes will not be recognized.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Pump
steps.instructions: Turn off the pump completely, wait 10–15 seconds, and power it back on. Reinsert the cassette and observe if it is recognized. Why: Temporary sensor or software errors can clear with a power cycle.

steps.title: Try a Known Good Cassette/Set
steps.instructions: If available, insert a different, verified functional cassette to see if the pump recognizes it. Why: Confirms whether the issue is with the pump or the cassette itself.

CCR.Complaint: Pump does not recognize cassette/IV set.
CCR.Cause: Sensor area clean, cassette correctly oriented; verified known-good cassette recognized—original set may be damaged or incompatible.
CCR.Resolution: Original cassette removed from service; patient switched to verified pump; pump labeled Out of Service and sent for repair.

title: Baxter Spectrum IQ Infusion Pump - Drug Library Sync Failure
description: Guide to troubleshoot Baxter Spectrum IQ infusion pump when it fails to synchronize the drug library.

steps.title: Verify Pump Power and Connectivity
steps.instructions: Ensure the pump is powered on and fully operational. Confirm the pump is connected to the hospital network (wired or Wi-Fi). A pump that is offline cannot sync with the drug library.

steps.title: Check Network Status
steps.instructions: Verify the network port or Wi-Fi signal strength. Check for network outages in the unit. Connectivity issues are the most common cause of failed synchronization.

steps.title: Validate Server/Management System
steps.instructions: Ensure the central Spectrum IQ management server is operational. Check that the drug library database is accessible and not locked by another process. If the server is down or database access is restricted, the pump cannot receive updates.

steps.title: Check Date/Time Settings on Pump
steps.instructions: Confirm the pump’s clock is synchronized with the network. Mismatched time can prevent secure communications and synchronization.

steps.title: Attempt Manual Sync
steps.instructions: Use the pump’s menu to initiate a manual drug library sync. Observe for success or specific error codes. Manual sync can bypass automated schedule errors and provide diagnostic codes.

steps.title: Review Error Codes and Logs
steps.instructions: Document any error codes displayed during sync attempts. Reference Baxter’s error code guide if available. Codes often indicate whether the problem is network, server, or pump-specific.

CCR.Complaint: Pump displays drug library sync failure; new drug profiles not available.
CCR.Cause: Network and server connectivity verified; pump date/time correct; manual sync failed with error code [insert code].
CCR.Resolution: Pump removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent to Biomedical Engineering for service evaluation.

title: Baxter Spectrum IQ Infusion Pump - Network Disconnects
description: Guide for troubleshooting intermittent or persistent network disconnects on Baxter Spectrum IQ infusion pumps.

steps.title: Verify Physical Network Connection
steps.instructions: Check that the Ethernet cable or docking station connection is secure. Look for visible damage to cables or connectors. Why: Loose or damaged cables are the most common cause of network disconnects.

steps.title: Confirm Network Port and Switch Status
steps.instructions: Ensure the network port used by the pump is active (check link lights). Test the port with a known working device if possible. Why: A disabled or faulty switch port can cause intermittent connectivity.

steps.title: Check Wi-Fi Connectivity (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Verify the pump is within range of the hospital’s Wi-Fi network. Confirm network SSID and security credentials are correct. Restart the Wi-Fi module if supported by the device.

steps.title: Restart the Pump and Network Equipment
steps.instructions: Power cycle the pump and confirm it reconnects to the network. Restart the associated docking station, Spectrum Gateway, or network switch if network-wide issues are suspected.

steps.title: Verify Network Configuration
steps.instructions: Confirm DHCP or static IP settings match hospital network requirements. Check that no IP conflicts exist.

steps.title: Observe Connection Stability
steps.instructions: Monitor the pump for repeated disconnects after performing the above steps. Record the duration and frequency of any disconnects.

CCR.Complaint: Pump frequently disconnects from the network, preventing remote monitoring.
CCR.Cause: Ethernet cable verified intact; network port active; pump fails to maintain connection after power cycle.
CCR.Resolution: Pump removed from service and sent for repair; patient transferred to a functional pump.

title: Baxter Spectrum IQ Infusion Pump - Air-in-Line False Alarms
description: Guide for troubleshooting false air-in-line alarms on Baxter Spectrum IQ infusion pumps.

steps.title: Verify Proper Priming of the Line
steps.instructions: Ensure the infusion line has been fully primed according to Baxter protocols. Air pockets near the syringe, bag, or tubing connectors can trigger false alarms. Incomplete priming is the most common cause of air-in-line alarms.

steps.title: Inspect Tubing Connections
steps.instructions: Check all connections from the pump to the patient for leaks or loose fittings. Ensure luer locks and clamps are secure. Loose connections can allow air to enter or create sensor misreads.

steps.title: Check for Kinks or Occlusions
steps.instructions: Examine the tubing for sharp bends, twists, or partial occlusions. Straighten or replace tubing if necessary. Kinks can create microbubbles or change flow dynamics, triggering sensors.

steps.title: Verify Sensor Cleanliness and Positioning
steps.instructions: Inspect the air-in-line sensor (usually optical) for condensation, residue, or obstruction. Clean the sensor per manufacturer instructions using approved wipes. Ensure tubing sits properly in the sensor window. Contaminants or misaligned tubing can cause false detection.

steps.title: Confirm Pump Software and Drug Library
steps.instructions: Verify that the pump is running the latest firmware version. Ensure the drug library is current and matches pump configuration. Software anomalies or mismatched library settings can occasionally trigger alarms.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Pump
steps.instructions: Turn the pump off, wait 10–15 seconds, then power it back on. Resets sensors and clears transient errors.

CCR.Complaint: Pump repeatedly alarms for air-in-line despite properly primed IV line.
CCR.Cause: External checks revealed tubing was correctly installed and primed; sensor clean; no actual air detected.
CCR.Resolution: Pump removed from service; sent to Biomedical Engineering for bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether alarms occurred during priming or infusion
Any recurring patterns (e.g., only certain bag sizes or line types)

title: Baxter Spectrum IQ Infusion Pump - Keypad/Button Failure
description: Guide to troubleshoot unresponsive or malfunctioning keypad/buttons on the Baxter Spectrum IQ infusion pump, focusing on safe external checks before internal repair.

steps.title: Ensure Pump is Powered Properly
steps.instructions: Check that the pump is plugged in or fully charged. Verify battery connection if in battery mode. Why: Keypads may not function correctly with low or unstable power.

steps.title: Inspect Keypad for Physical Obstruction
steps.instructions: Look for dirt, fluids, or debris on/around the buttons. Wipe gently with a soft, lint-free cloth dampened with approved cleaning solution. Why: Residue can prevent proper button actuation.

steps.title: Check for Moisture or Liquid Exposure
steps.instructions: Confirm that the pump has not been exposed to liquid spills or cleaning overspray. Dry keypad area if moisture is present. Why: Excess moisture can cause intermittent keypad behavior.

steps.title: Verify Protective Cover / Overlay
steps.instructions: If a silicone or plastic overlay is used, remove it and test buttons. Why: Some overlays can shift and block proper button travel.

steps.title: Perform a Soft Reset / Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Power down the pump completely, wait 10–15 seconds, and power it back on. Why: Software glitches can cause unresponsive buttons.

steps.title: Test All Buttons
steps.instructions: Methodically press each key to determine if failure is isolated or across the entire keypad. Note any buttons that are unresponsive or inconsistent.

steps.title: Check Device Logs or Error Messages
steps.instructions: Some Spectrum IQ models record keypad errors in the event log. Why: Confirms if device recognizes button failures.

CCR.Complaint: Pump keypad unresponsive; buttons do not register inputs.
CCR.Cause: Keypad unresponsive after visual inspection and power cycle; no debris or moisture found; software reset did not resolve issue.
CCR.Resolution: Pump removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for manufacturer evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Battery status and last charging cycle
Any unusual keypad behavior (sticking, repeated inputs)
Device location at time of failure
Error codes or logs from the Spectrum IQ

title: Baxter Spectrum IQ Infusion Pump - Charging Dock Communication Errors
description: Guide for troubleshooting communication errors between Baxter Spectrum IQ pumps and their charging docks.

steps.title: Inspect Dock and Pump Connectors
steps.instructions: Check for debris, corrosion, or bent pins on both the pump and dock connectors. Rationale: Dirty or misaligned connectors often cause communication failures.

steps.title: Verify Proper Seating
steps.instructions: Ensure the pump is fully seated in the dock. Docking incorrectly can prevent communication.

steps.title: Check Dock Power Supply
steps.instructions: Confirm the dock is plugged into a live outlet. Test the outlet with a known-working device. Check the dock’s power LED indicator (if present) for normal operation.

steps.title: Swap Cables or Docks (if available)
steps.instructions: If the hospital has another compatible dock or cable, try swapping to rule out a faulty dock.

steps.title: Restart Pump and Dock
steps.instructions: Remove the pump, power it off, wait 30 seconds, then power back on. If dock has a power cycle option, do the same. Rationale: Temporary software or communication glitches can clear after a restart.

steps.title: Check for Firmware Updates
steps.instructions: Confirm the pump firmware is up to date. Some communication issues resolve with current software.

steps.title: Observe Indicator Lights / Error Codes
steps.instructions: Note any blinking lights or error messages during docking. This may provide clues to the cause.

CCR.Complaint: Pump displays ‘Charging Dock Not Recognized’ when docked.
CCR.Cause: Pump seated correctly; dock powered; connector pins inspected; communication still fails.
CCR.Resolution: Pump removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for repair. Dock also evaluated for faults.

helpfulDetails: Any error codes or LED patterns observed

title: Baxter PrismaFlex - Cartridge or Filter Set Not Detected
description: Troubleshooting cartridge or filter detection issues on the PrismaFlex CRRT system.

steps.title: Verify Correct Cartridge/Filter Set
steps.instructions: Confirm the installed set is compatible with the PrismaFlex system. Incorrect or expired sets may not be recognized by the machine.

steps.title: Check Proper Cartridge Installation
steps.instructions: Ensure the cartridge is fully seated in the mounting area. Look for misalignment, gaps, or incomplete engagement with the holder. Improper seating can prevent detection sensors from activating.

steps.title: Inspect Cartridge Detection Area
steps.instructions: Check for debris, fluid, or residue in the cartridge bay. Clean gently if needed. Sensors may fail to detect the cartridge if obstructed.

steps.title: Verify Door Closure and Latching
steps.instructions: Ensure the cartridge compartment door is fully closed and latched. Many systems require proper door engagement to confirm cartridge presence.

steps.title: Check for Damaged Cartridge Components
steps.instructions: Inspect plastic housing, connectors, or identification features (e.g., tabs or chips). A damaged cartridge may not be detected.

steps.title: Reseat the Cartridge
steps.instructions: Remove and reinstall the cartridge carefully. Listen/feel for proper engagement. This often resolves alignment-related detection issues.

steps.title: Restart the System
steps.instructions: Power cycle the PrismaFlex unit. Restarting can reset detection logic and clear false alarms.

steps.title: Check Cable Connections (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Some configurations may include sensor or communication points near the cartridge area. Ensure no loose or disconnected wiring externally visible.

steps.title: Try a Different Cartridge Set
steps.instructions: Install a new, known-good cartridge. This helps determine if the issue is with the disposable set or the machine.

steps.title: Observe for Error Messages or Patterns
steps.instructions: Note if the system intermittently detects the cartridge. Intermittent detection may indicate sensor alignment or wear issues.

CCR.Complaint: Unit reports “Cartridge Not Detected” and will not start therapy.
CCR.Cause: Cartridge not fully seated in holder; detection sensor not engaged.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated cartridge and verified proper installation; system recognized cartridge and passed setup.

helpfulDetails: Cartridge type and lot number checked
Final device status (returned to service or removed)

title: Baxter PrismaFlex - Air Bubble Detector Alarms
description: Troubleshooting air bubble detector alarms on the PrismaFlex CRRT system with focus on setup, connections, and fluid path issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Never troubleshoot while the patient is actively connected to the device. Transition the patient to another machine before proceeding. Air bubble alarms are a critical safety mechanism to prevent air embolism.

steps.title: Verify the alarm message and location
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the alarm is identifying air in the arterial line, venous return line, or within the cartridge. This helps narrow down where the issue is occurring in the circuit.

steps.title: Check for visible air in the bloodlines
steps.instructions: Inspect all tubing segments for air bubbles or foam. Even small bubbles can trigger sensitive air detectors.

steps.title: Ensure proper priming of the circuit
steps.instructions: Confirm the circuit was fully primed according to procedure. Incomplete priming is one of the most common causes of repeated air alarms.

steps.title: Inspect all connections and luer locks
steps.instructions: Check for loose, cracked, or improperly seated connections. Air can be introduced into the system through even minor leaks.

steps.title: Check fluid bags and lines for depletion or air introduction
steps.instructions: Ensure replacement fluids and dialysate bags are not empty and lines are properly submerged. Air may enter if a bag runs dry or tubing is not properly positioned.

steps.title: Verify proper placement of tubing in the air detector
steps.instructions: Ensure the tubing is correctly seated in the air bubble detector clamp. Misalignment can cause false alarms.

steps.title: Clean the air detector sensor area
steps.instructions: Inspect for moisture, residue, or debris on the sensor window. Contamination can interfere with ultrasonic detection.

steps.title: Check for kinks or occlusions in the tubing
steps.instructions: Kinks can cause turbulent flow and introduce microbubbles that trigger alarms.

steps.title: Observe for excessive foaming in the circuit
steps.instructions: Foam can be interpreted as air by the detector. This may indicate blood-air interface issues or improper setup.

steps.title: Power cycle the system (if clinically appropriate)
steps.instructions: Restart the machine to rule out a transient sensor or software issue. Only perform this step when the patient is safely off therapy.

CCR.Complaint: Unit alarming ‘Air Bubble Detected’ and unable to continue therapy.
CCR.Cause: Air observed in venous return line due to incomplete priming and loose luer connection.
CCR.Resolution: Re-primed circuit, secured all connections, verified no air present, and confirmed normal operation.

helpfulDetails: No visible air or foam present
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Baxter PrismaFlex - Effluent Scale or Weight Sensor Calibration Errors
description: Troubleshooting guide for resolving effluent scale and weight sensor calibration errors on the PrismaFlex CRRT system.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Ensure the system is not connected to a patient before troubleshooting. Calibration and scale checks must be performed safely.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions on the Effluent Scale
steps.instructions: Confirm nothing is touching or resting on the effluent scale except the designated effluent bag. Even slight contact can cause calibration errors.

steps.title: Inspect Effluent Bag Placement
steps.instructions: Ensure the effluent bag is properly hung, freely hanging, and not touching any surfaces or overfilled.

steps.title: Verify Scale and Environment
steps.instructions: Ensure the unit is on a level surface, wheels are locked, and there is no vibration or airflow affecting the scale.

steps.title: Run Calibration
steps.instructions: Perform a system restart and run scale calibration per on-screen prompts without disturbing the machine.

CCR.Complaint: Unit alarming for effluent scale calibration failure; unable to start therapy.
CCR.Cause: No external obstructions found; calibration repeatedly failed despite proper setup and stable environment, indicating likely internal scale fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service, labeled out of service, and sent to vendor/biomed shop for scale evaluation and repair.

helpfulDetails: Effluent bag properly hung and not touching surfaces
Unit on level surface with wheels locked
Alarm messages or error codes recorded

title: Baxter PrismaFlex - Blood Leak Detector Faults
description: Guide for troubleshooting blood leak detector faults on the PrismaFlex CRRT system by ruling out setup, contamination, and flow issues before internal failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the device is not actively connected to a patient. If in use, ensure clinical staff has safely transitioned the patient to another machine before proceeding.

steps.title: Verify the Alarm and Message Details
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm text and when it occurs (startup, during therapy, or intermittently). This helps determine if the issue is setup-related or develops during operation.

steps.title: Inspect the Effluent Line for Proper Installation
steps.instructions: Verify the effluent line is correctly routed through the blood leak detector. Ensure it is fully seated and aligned in the detector slot. Misalignment can cause false readings.

steps.title: Check for Kinks, Air, or Improper Fluid Flow
steps.instructions: Inspect the effluent tubing for kinks or occlusions. Look for air bubbles or inconsistent flow. Irregular flow can interfere with optical detection.

steps.title: Inspect the Effluent Fluid for Discoloration
steps.instructions: Check if the effluent appears pink or red (possible true blood leak). If discoloration is present, this may indicate a filter membrane failure rather than a sensor issue.

steps.title: Clean the Blood Leak Detector Sensor Area
steps.instructions: Open the detector and inspect for residue, dried fluid, or condensation. Clean the optical surfaces using approved wipes (lint-free, non-abrasive). Even minor residue can interfere with light transmission.

steps.title: Check for Moisture or Condensation
steps.instructions: Moisture inside the detector housing can cause false alarms. Allow the area to dry completely before retesting.

steps.title: Verify Proper Cartridge/Filter Set Installation
steps.instructions: Ensure the correct cartridge is installed and fully seated. Improper seating can affect flow characteristics and detection accuracy.

steps.title: Run a System Self-Test (If Available)
steps.instructions: Perform the PrismaFlex self-test or diagnostic check. Observe if the blood leak detector passes or fails during testing.

steps.title: Swap with a Known Good Set (If Available)
steps.instructions: Replace the cartridge and effluent line with a known good setup. This helps isolate whether the issue is disposables-related versus device-related.

steps.title: Observe for Recurring Alarms
steps.instructions: If the alarm persists after cleaning and setup verification, suspect sensor degradation or internal calibration issues.

CCR.Complaint: User reported blood leak alarm preventing therapy initiation on PrismaFlex.
CCR.Cause: Dried effluent residue and moisture buildup observed in blood leak detector sensor causing false optical readings.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned and dried detector sensor area, verified proper tubing alignment, and tested unit. Alarm cleared and device returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Effluent line properly seated and aligned

title: Baxter PrismaFlex - Pump Module or Pressure Sensor Failures
description: Troubleshooting pump module and pressure sensor issues on the PrismaFlex CRRT system, emphasizing setup and external causes before internal failure.

steps.title: Verify System Power and Startup Status
steps.instructions: Ensure the unit powers on normally with no boot errors. Confirm all modules initialize without faults. Why: Startup issues can mimic module or sensor failures.

steps.title: Check for Active Alarms and Error Codes
steps.instructions: Review displayed alarms (arterial/venous pressure, TMP, access pressure). Document exact alarm messages. Why: Specific alarms help determine whether the issue is sensor-related or flow-related.

steps.title: Inspect Cartridge Installation
steps.instructions: Ensure the cartridge is properly seated and fully locked into place. Verify tubing is correctly routed through pump segments and pressure pods. Why: Misalignment can prevent accurate pressure sensing or proper pump operation.

steps.title: Check Tubing Placement in Pump Segments
steps.instructions: Confirm tubing is correctly loaded in all pump heads (blood, effluent, replacement). Ensure there is no twisting, kinking, or partial disengagement. Why: Improper loading affects pump rotation and pressure readings.

steps.title: Inspect Pressure Sensor Pods and Interfaces
steps.instructions: Verify pressure lines are properly connected and seated in their designated ports. Check for fluid contamination, moisture, or debris in pressure pod areas. Why: Contaminated or improperly seated sensors can cause false readings or alarms.

steps.title: Check for Air in Lines
steps.instructions: Inspect all lines for visible air bubbles, especially near pressure monitoring points. Prime the system if necessary. Why: Air can distort pressure readings and trigger alarms.

steps.title: Verify Line Patency and Flow Path
steps.instructions: Check for clamps left closed, occlusions, or kinked tubing. Ensure smooth flow through the circuit. Why: Flow restrictions can trigger pressure alarms that appear sensor-related.

steps.title: Swap Cartridge (If Available)
steps.instructions: Replace with a known-good cartridge set. Why: Eliminates disposable-related defects affecting pump or pressure readings.

steps.title: Inspect for External Signs of Pump Issues
steps.instructions: Listen for abnormal noises (grinding, slipping, inconsistent rotation). Observe pump operation during setup or priming. Why: Mechanical pump issues may be externally noticeable.

steps.title: Perform System Restart
steps.instructions: Power cycle the unit and reattempt setup. Why: Clears temporary faults or sensor communication errors.

steps.title: Run Self-Test or Diagnostic (If Available)
steps.instructions: Use built-in diagnostics to evaluate pump and pressure sensor function. Why: Confirms whether the system detects internal faults.

CCR.Complaint: Unit alarming with continuous arterial pressure errors and unable to start therapy.
CCR.Cause: Improper tubing placement and pressure line not fully seated in sensor port, causing inaccurate readings.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated tubing and pressure connections, verified proper cartridge installation, and confirmed normal operation during setup.

helpfulDetails: Outlet tested and system powered normally
Pump noise or abnormal operation noted
Final device status (in service or removed)

title: Baxter Novum IQ - Network Connectivity or EMR Sync Failure
description: Troubleshooting guide for Novum IQ infusion pump network connectivity and EMR synchronization failures.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot on a pump actively infusing a patient. Move the patient to another verified pump before troubleshooting. Only perform connectivity troubleshooting when patient safety is ensured.

steps.title: Check Pump Power and Status
steps.instructions: Ensure the pump is powered on and not in a “standby” or “error” state. Confirm battery is adequately charged or connected to AC power. Why: A powered-down pump cannot communicate with the network.

steps.title: Verify Network Cable or Wi-Fi Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the network cable for damage and ensure it is securely connected. If using Wi-Fi, confirm the pump is connected to the correct SSID. Why: Loose or damaged cables or incorrect Wi-Fi credentials prevent network communication.

steps.title: Confirm IP Address and Network Settings
steps.instructions: Check the pump’s network configuration menu for a valid IP address, subnet mask, and gateway. Ensure DHCP is functioning or the static IP does not conflict with another device. Why: Network misconfiguration is a common cause of EMR sync failures.

steps.title: Check Hospital Network Status
steps.instructions: Verify that the network switch, access point, or port the pump is connected to is operational. Confirm no recent network maintenance or firewall changes are blocking communication. Why: External network issues can appear as pump connectivity problems.

steps.title: Confirm EMR Integration Settings
steps.instructions: Ensure the pump is registered in the EMR system with correct identifiers. Verify that the integration server or middleware (e.g., Smart Pump Interface) is operational. Why: If the server or integration settings are misconfigured, data will not sync.

steps.title: Restart Pump and/or Network Connection
steps.instructions: Reboot the pump and, if possible, reset the network connection. Observe whether the pump successfully reconnects to the EMR. Why: Some transient network errors are resolved with a reboot.

CCR.Complaint: Pump not showing infusion data in EMR; network connection failure alarm present.
CCR.Cause: Observed pump powered on, connected to network, valid IP, but EMR integration server unreachable.
CCR.Resolution: Restarted pump and network connection; issue persisted. Pump removed from service and sent for bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Final pump status (Out of Service / In Service)

title: Baxter Novum IQ - Drug Library Not Loading or Corrupted
description: Troubleshooting guide for Novum IQ infusion pump drug library loading or corruption issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot while the pump is actively infusing a patient. Move the patient to a properly functioning pump before proceeding.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Power on the pump and attempt to access the drug library. Note any error messages (e.g., “Library Not Found,” “Library Corrupt,” or sync errors). Confirm whether the issue is consistent or intermittent.

steps.title: Check Correct Care Area/Profile Selection
steps.instructions: Ensure the correct care unit or profile is selected on the pump. Some libraries are unit-specific and may appear missing if the wrong profile is chosen.

steps.title: Confirm Network Connectivity
steps.instructions: Verify the pump is connected to Wi-Fi or the hospital network. Check for network indicators or connection status on the device. Move the pump to a known good coverage area if needed.

steps.title: Force Library Sync / Refresh
steps.instructions: Attempt to manually sync or refresh the drug library from the settings menu. Reboot the pump and allow time for automatic synchronization.

steps.title: Check Server / Integration Status
steps.instructions: Confirm with IT or clinical systems that the drug library server or integration platform is operational. Verify there are no ongoing outages or deployment issues.

steps.title: Compare with Another Pump
steps.instructions: Check a known working Novum IQ pump on the same unit. Verify if the drug library loads correctly on that device.

steps.title: Inspect for Incomplete Updates
steps.instructions: Determine if a recent drug library update was pushed. Look for signs of interrupted updates (battery loss during update, network drop).

steps.title: Check Date and Time Settings
steps.instructions: Ensure the system date and time are correct.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Reboot
steps.instructions: Power cycle the pump fully and allow it to reconnect and resync.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported infusion pump drug library would not load; pump displayed “Library Not Found” and could not start medication using guardrails.
CCR.Cause: Pump was not connected to the network and failed to sync the drug library. After reconnecting, library still failed to load, suggesting incomplete or corrupted update.
CCR.Resolution: Attempted network reconnection, forced sync, and reboot with no success. Removed pump from service and sent to vendor for software reload.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (removed from service or returned to use)

title: Baxter Novum IQ - Occlusion Alarm Troubleshooting
description: Guide for troubleshooting occlusion alarms on the Baxter Novum IQ infusion pump by identifying and resolving external flow restrictions before escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot while the pump is actively infusing a patient. Ensure the patient is safely transitioned to another functioning device before continuing.

steps.title: Confirm the Alarm Condition
steps.instructions: Verify the exact alarm message (e.g., upstream vs downstream occlusion). This helps determine whether the restriction is before or after the pump mechanism.

steps.title: Check for Closed or Partially Closed Clamps
steps.instructions: Inspect all roller clamps and slide clamps on the IV tubing. A partially closed clamp is one of the most common causes of occlusion alarms.

steps.title: Inspect the IV Tubing for Kinks or Obstructions
steps.instructions: Trace the tubing from the bag to the patient. Look for kinks or tight bends and compression under bed rails or equipment. Straighten or reposition as needed.

steps.title: Verify Proper Tubing Installation in the Pump
steps.instructions: Ensure the administration set is correctly seated in the pump channel and fully loaded according to manufacturer guidance. Misalignment can create artificial resistance.

steps.title: Check the IV Site (If Applicable)
steps.instructions: Coordinate with clinical staff to assess infiltration, catheter occlusion, and patient positioning affecting flow. Resistance at the patient site will trigger downstream occlusion alarms.

steps.title: Inspect the Fluid Bag and Upstream Flow
steps.instructions: Ensure the fluid bag is not empty. Confirm the spike is properly inserted and the drip chamber is correctly filled. Check for collapsed bags (especially with non-vented sets).

steps.title: Verify Correct Administration Set Type
steps.instructions: Ensure the tubing set is compatible with the Novum IQ. Incorrect or non-approved sets may cause flow resistance or sensor misinterpretation.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Pump
steps.instructions: Turn the pump off and back on. This can clear temporary sensor or software-related faults.

steps.title: Test with a Known Good Setup
steps.instructions: Replace tubing with a new, known good administration set. Run a controlled test infusion (per policy) to see if the alarm persists. This helps isolate whether the issue is setup-related or device-related.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported frequent occlusion alarms during infusion despite proper setup.
CCR.Cause: Kinked tubing found under bed rail causing intermittent downstream occlusion.
CCR.Resolution: Repositioned tubing, verified proper flow, and confirmed pump operation without further alarms.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Baxter Novum IQ - Air-in-Line Detector Errors
description: Troubleshooting air-in-line detector errors on the Baxter Novum IQ infusion pump with a focus on external causes and safe escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the pump is not actively infusing on a patient, transfer the patient to a functioning device if needed, and recognize that air-in-line conditions can pose serious risk if ignored.

steps.title: Verify the Alarm Message and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed (air-in-line, air detected, or similar) and note if the alarm is continuous or intermittent to determine whether the issue is actual air vs. detection inconsistency.

steps.title: Inspect the IV Tubing for Air Bubbles
steps.instructions: Visually inspect the entire fluid path from bag to patient, look for visible air bubbles, gaps, or foam, and remove air by priming the line per hospital protocol if present.

steps.title: Check for Improper Priming
steps.instructions: Confirm the tubing was fully primed before connection and re-prime if necessary since partial priming can leave microbubbles that trigger alarms.

steps.title: Verify Correct Tubing Type and Setup
steps.instructions: Ensure the correct Baxter-approved tubing set is being used and that the tubing is properly loaded in the pump channel, as misalignment can interfere with the sensor.

steps.title: Inspect the Air-in-Line Sensor Area
steps.instructions: Open the pump door and inspect the sensor region for residue, dried fluid, condensation, debris, or dust, and clean per approved procedures if contamination is present.

steps.title: Check for Kinks or Irregular Flow
steps.instructions: Inspect tubing for kinks, compression, or sharp bends and ensure clamps are fully open since irregular flow can introduce air pockets or trigger detection errors.

steps.title: Swap Tubing Set
steps.instructions: Replace the IV tubing with a new, properly primed set to rule out defective or compromised tubing.

steps.title: Test with a Known Good Setup
steps.instructions: Set up the pump with a test fluid bag and new tubing and run a controlled infusion to determine if the alarm persists.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Pump
steps.instructions: Turn the pump off and back on and re-test with proper setup to clear temporary sensor or software faults.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported repeated air-in-line alarms during infusion despite no visible air in tubing.
CCR.Cause: Observed no air in line; sensor area had visible residue causing intermittent false detection.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned sensor area, replaced tubing, and tested with known good setup. Alarm cleared and unit returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Tubing fully primed and free of visible air
Pump tested with known good setup

title: Baxter Novum IQ - Battery or Power Module Failure
description: Troubleshooting guide for resolving battery and power-related issues on the Baxter Novum IQ infusion pump.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot on a device connected to a patient. Transition the patient to a properly functioning infusion pump before removing this device from service.

steps.title: Verify AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Confirm the outlet is functional using a known working device. Check for switched outlets or power strips that may be turned off. Why: Many “no power” issues are caused by unavailable or interrupted AC power.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and Adapter
steps.instructions: Ensure the correct Baxter-approved power cord is being used. Check for visible damage (cuts, bent prongs, loose connections). Reseat both ends of the cord firmly. Why: Damaged or loose cords can prevent charging or powering.

steps.title: Check Power Indicator Lights
steps.instructions: Observe if any LEDs illuminate when plugged into AC power. Look for charging indicators or fault lights. Why: Indicator behavior helps differentiate between battery vs. internal power failure.

steps.title: Attempt Power-On with AC Connected
steps.instructions: Plug the pump into AC power and attempt to power on. If the unit powers on only when plugged in, suspect battery degradation. Why: This isolates battery function from the main power supply.

steps.title: Check Battery Status on Screen (if accessible)
steps.instructions: Navigate to system/battery status if the device powers on. Look for: Low capacity warnings, Charging status, Battery not detected messages. Why: Provides direct feedback on battery health and recognition.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Compartment (External Only)
steps.instructions: If accessible without tools, verify battery is properly seated. Look for corrosion, swelling, or damage. Do not open the device or access internal components. Why: Poor battery contact can prevent proper operation.

steps.title: Swap with Known Good Power Cord or Battery (if available)
steps.instructions: Replace the power cord with a known working one. If batteries are user-replaceable, try a known good battery. Why: Helps isolate whether the issue is accessory-related vs. device-related.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Power Loss
steps.instructions: Gently move the power cord at the connection point. Observe for power drops or flickering. Why: May indicate a worn power port or loose connection.

steps.title: Listen and Observe During Charging
steps.instructions: Check for unusual heat, noise, or burning smell. Why: These may indicate internal power supply issues and require immediate removal from service.

CCR.Complaint: Pump would not power on and displayed battery failure alarm during use.
CCR.Cause: Battery not holding charge and device only powered on when connected to AC. Verified with known good outlet and power cord.
CCR.Resolution: Removed pump from service and sent for battery replacement and internal power module evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Power cord condition and swap performed
Device powers on with AC only vs. battery
Presence of heat, odor, or noise

steps.instructions: Confirm Error 100 PIC Error appears and determine when it occurs and associated behavior.

steps.title: Power Reset
steps.instructions: Fully power off, disconnect from AC, allow discharge, and restart the pump.

steps.title: Check Power and Battery
steps.instructions: Verify stable AC power, charging indicators, and absence of battery issues.

steps.title: Inspect Physical Condition
steps.instructions: Check for damage, fluid intrusion, and loose connectors.

steps.title: Functional Test
steps.instructions: Attempt menu navigation and test infusion off patient to check for recurring error.

CCR.Complaint: Pump displaying Error 100 PIC Error and unable to initiate infusion.
CCR.Cause: Persistent PIC communication error after verifying power and performing reset.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent for repair evaluation.

title: Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pump - Error: 102 PIC Not Responding
description: Error: 102 PIC Not Responding indicates internal controller communication failure causing pump unresponsiveness or inability to deliver infusion.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop infusion immediately, remove pump from use, and transfer patient to a backup infusion device.

steps.title: Verify Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm Error: 102 PIC Not Responding is displayed and observe responsiveness, boot behavior, and consistency.

steps.title: Perform Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Turn off the pump, disconnect power, wait 30–60 seconds, and restart.

steps.title: Check Power Source and Battery
steps.instructions: Use a known good outlet, verify charging indicators, and test with and without AC power.

steps.title: Inspect External Condition
steps.instructions: Check for fluid ingress, physical damage, or signs of impact.

steps.title: Attempt Alternate Startup Conditions
steps.instructions: Remove accessories and attempt startup in minimal configuration.

steps.title: Evaluate Failure Consistency
steps.instructions: Repeat power cycles to determine if the error is intermittent or persistent.

CCR.Complaint: Pump displaying Error: 102 PIC Not Responding and unresponsive to inputs.
CCR.Cause: Internal communication failure suspected after power cycling and external checks did not resolve the issue.

steps.instructions: Confirm Error 105 PIC Motor Error is displayed and observe behavior.

steps.title: Inspect Mechanical Path

steps.title: Check Door and Latch
steps.instructions: Ensure proper closure and latch integrity.

steps.title: Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Restart the pump after disconnecting power briefly.

steps.title: Isolate Setup
steps.instructions: Test with different tubing and outlet or compare with another pump.

CCR.Complaint: Pump displaying Error 105 PIC Motor Error and unable to start infusion.

title: Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pump - Error: 106 PIC Index Error

steps.title: Check Set Loading

steps.title: Inspect for Obstructions
steps.instructions: Visually check for debris or blockage in the pump mechanism.

steps.title: Power Cycle

steps.title: Test with Known-Good Set
steps.instructions: Install a different IV set to rule out accessory issues.

steps.title: Observe for Recurrence
steps.instructions: Monitor pump operation for repeat errors or failure to run.

CCR.Complaint: Pump displaying Error 106 PIC Index Error; infusion would not start.

title: Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pump - Error: 107 PIC CAM Error

CCR.Complaint: Pump displaying Error 107 PIC CAM Error; unable to start infusion.

title: Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pump - Error: 109 PIC Encoder Error

steps.instructions: Verify tubing, loading, and absence of occlusions.

steps.title: Reattempt Infusion
steps.instructions: Restart infusion and confirm normal operation.

CCR.Complaint: Pump displaying Error 111 Pulse dmnd too high during interval infusion.

title: Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pump - Error: 210 SPI PIC Timeout
description: Error 210 SPI PIC Timeout indicates loss of communication between internal control components, leading to pump stoppage or failure to start infusion.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop infusion immediately, remove pump from patient use, and transfer therapy to a functioning pump.

steps.title: Verify Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm Error 210 is displayed and observe when it occurs and how the pump behaves.

steps.title: Perform Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Turn off pump, disconnect power, wait 30–60 seconds, and restart.

steps.title: Check Power and Battery
steps.instructions: Use a known good outlet and verify battery condition and charging status.

steps.title: Inspect External Connections
steps.instructions: Reseat modules and inspect connectors for contamination or damage.

steps.title: Test Minimal Configuration
steps.instructions: Power on without accessories to determine if issue is externally influenced.

CCR.Complaint: Pump displaying Error 210 SPI PIC Timeout; unable to start infusion.
CCR.Cause: Internal communication failure suspected after power cycle and external checks did not resolve issue.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for repair/bench evaluation.

title: Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pump - Error: 212 Sensor Task Timeout
description: Error 212 Sensor Task Timeout indicates a failure in internal sensor processing, causing infusion interruption or unsafe operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop infusion, remove pump from use, and transfer therapy to another device.

steps.title: Verify Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm Error 212 is displayed and observe when and how it occurs.

steps.title: Perform Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Turn off, remove power, wait 30–60 seconds, and restart the pump.

steps.title: Check Power Stability
steps.instructions: Test operation on AC and battery power to rule out voltage instability.

steps.title: Inspect External Components
steps.instructions: Check cassette area, connections, and housing for damage or contamination.

steps.title: Test with Known-Good Setup
steps.instructions: Load a known-good IV set and test pump operation off-patient.

steps.title: Remove from Service
steps.instructions: If error persists, remove from service and send for repair.

CCR.Complaint: Pump displaying Error 212 Sensor Task Timeout and stopping infusion during use
CCR.Cause: Internal sensor task timing failure; error persisted after power cycle and external checks
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service, labeled out of service, and sent to vendor/biomed repair for evaluation

title: Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pump - Error: 213 Battery Task Timeout
description: Error 213 Battery Task Timeout indicating battery communication or power management failure causing alarms or pump operation issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove pump from use and transfer infusion to a working device.

steps.title: Verify Error and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm error appears and note when it occurs and associated symptoms.

steps.title: Check AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Plug into known-good outlet and verify charging indicator.

steps.title: Inspect Power Adapter
steps.instructions: Check for damage or loose connections and test with known-good adapter.

steps.title: Evaluate Battery Charging
steps.instructions: Observe charging behavior and test pump operation on battery.

steps.title: Reseat or Replace Battery
steps.instructions: Inspect contacts and install known-good battery if available.

steps.title: Perform Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Restart device after removing power for 30–60 seconds.

steps.title: Isolate Battery vs Internal Issue
steps.instructions: Determine if issue occurs only on battery or also on AC power.

CCR.Complaint: Pump displaying Error 213 Battery Task Timeout with intermittent power concerns.
CCR.Cause: Battery failure or internal battery communication timeout observed during testing.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced battery and tested operation; if unresolved, removed from service and sent for repair.

title: Baxter SIGMA Spectrum Infusion Pump - Error: 240 Key Buffer is Full
description: Error 240 Key Buffer is Full indicates excessive or continuous keypad input causing unresponsive controls, delayed entries, or unintended actions affecting pump operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First

steps.title: Check for Stuck Keys

steps.title: Power Cycle Pump

CCR.Complaint: Pump displaying Error 240 Key Buffer is Full with unresponsive keypad during programming.

CCR.Resolution: Cleaned keypad, checked for obstructions, and power cycled unit. Error persisted, device removed from service and sent for repair.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: Air in Blood
description: Troubleshooting the PrismaFlex Air in Blood alarm when air, foam, leakage, incomplete priming, or return-line installation issues affect the blood return path and interrupt CRRT therapy.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Treat the Air in Blood alarm as a patient safety issue. Do not bypass or ignore the alarm. Confirm the patient is clinically stable, therapy is paused or managed according to the device prompts and hospital policy, and clinical staff are involved before handling the blood path.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the PrismaFlex displays Alarm: Air in Blood. Identify when the alarm occurs and look for visible air or foam, air in the deaeration chamber, frequent return-line alarms, incomplete priming, return-line misplacement, leakage, or loose disposable-set connections.

steps.title: Stop and Inspect Before Continuing
steps.instructions: Inspect the disposable set and blood path for disconnected tubing, loose luer connections, leaking access connections, cracked disposable components, air or foam in the return line, air in the deaeration chamber, and incomplete priming.

steps.title: Check the Blood Access and Disposable Set for Leaks
steps.instructions: Inspect the blood access connection and disposable set. Verify access and return line connections are secure, tubing is not disconnected, no leakage is present, and no loose caps, damaged fittings, or open connections are present.

steps.title: Verify Return Line Placement in the Air Detector
steps.instructions: Confirm the return line is fully seated in the PrismaFlex air detector, not twisted or pinched, not partially outside the sensor path, and that the detector door or tubing holder is properly closed.

steps.title: Inspect for Visible Air or Foam
steps.instructions: Inspect the return line and deaeration chamber for air bubbles, foam, trapped air, air moving through the blood path, or air distributed throughout the set. Localized air may be removable through the guided process, but widespread air requires set replacement.

steps.title: Follow the On-Screen Air Removal Process
steps.instructions: Follow the PrismaFlex on-screen instructions exactly. This may include obtaining negative return pressure, using RELEASE CLAMP when prompted, drawing blood into the return line or deaeration chamber, and removing visible air from the return path.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Set Was Fully Primed
steps.instructions: If the alarm occurred during setup, startup, or shortly after treatment began, check for incomplete priming, air remaining in tubing, air in the deaeration chamber, foam from improper priming, or incorrect line installation during setup.

steps.title: Replace the Set if Air Is Present Throughout the Circuit
steps.instructions: If air is visible throughout the disposable set, press DISCONNECT and remove the device from active therapy according to on-screen instructions and hospital policy. Load and prime a new disposable set before returning to therapy.

steps.title: Confirm Alarm Resolution
steps.instructions: Confirm the Air in Blood alarm has cleared, no visible air or foam remains, the return line is properly installed in the detector, all connections are secure, no leakage is present, and therapy resumes without repeated alarm.

CCR.Complaint: PrismaFlex CRRT reported Air in Blood alarm during treatment. Staff observed air or foam in the return path, and therapy could not continue until the alarm was corrected.
CCR.Cause: Air was detected in the blood return path. External inspection found possible disposable-set air source, return-line installation issue, incomplete priming, leakage, or air/foam in tubing. If unresolved after set replacement and proper setup, likely air detector or sensor-related failure.
CCR.Resolution: Inspected blood access and disposable set for leakage or disconnection. Verified return line placement in the air detector. Checked return tubing and deaeration chamber for visible air or foam. Followed on-screen air removal process using negative return pressure and RELEASE CLAMP when prompted. If air remained throughout the set, treatment was disconnected and a new set was loaded and primed. Device returned to service only after alarm cleared and no visible air remained. If alarm persisted, device was removed from service for bench evaluation.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: Return Disconnection
description: Troubleshooting guide for Baxter PrismaFlex Return Disconnection alarms involving low return pressure, possible return-line or catheter disconnection, chamber monitor-line issues, wet fluid barrier, tubing restriction, or pressure sensing failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Immediately assess whether the PrismaFlex is actively connected to a patient. If there is any concern for blood loss, return-line disconnection, catheter dislodgement, air exposure, or unsafe therapy delivery, stop therapy according to facility protocol and get clinical staff involved immediately.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the PrismaFlex displays Alarm: Return Disconnection. Observe whether the alarm occurs at startup, during active therapy, after patient movement, after line manipulation, or intermittently during treatment. Check for low or falling return pressure, visible disconnection, blood leakage, loose catheter connection, or recurring alarm behavior.

steps.title: Check the Return Line and Patient Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the full return blood path from the PrismaFlex return line to the patient catheter. Confirm the return line and catheter connections are secure, with no loose Luer connection, visible blood leakage, catheter hub separation, or line pulled loose by patient movement.

steps.title: Check for Patient or Line Movement
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff whether the patient recently moved, coughed, was repositioned, transported, or had the catheter or lines handled. Inspect for tension, pulling, twisting, unsupported tubing, or routing that strains the catheter connection.

steps.title: Check the Chamber Monitor Line Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the chamber monitor line and confirm it is connected properly. Look for a loose chamber monitor line, incorrect connection, line not fully seated, damaged connector, or moisture or blood in the monitor line.

steps.title: Inspect the Fluid Barrier
steps.instructions: Check the fluid barrier associated with the pressure monitoring path. Look for wet barrier, blood contamination, fluid migration into the pressure line, or moisture near the monitor connection. If the barrier is wet, use STOP and CHANGE SET to load and prime a new set according to facility procedure.

steps.title: Check for Obstruction Before the Deaeration Chamber
steps.instructions: Inspect the blood flow path before the deaeration chamber. Look for clamped tubing, kinked tubing, compressed tubing under the door or pump segment, improperly routed tubing, or flow restriction upstream of the deaeration chamber.

steps.title: Check External Setup and Accessories
steps.instructions: Verify the correct disposable set is loaded, tubing is seated correctly, pressure pod and monitor connections are secure, and no tubing damage, contamination, fluid, or debris is present at pressure interfaces or sensor connection areas.

steps.title: Restart or Continue Only if Safe and Allowed
steps.instructions: If the cause is corrected and clinical staff determine it is safe to proceed, follow PrismaFlex prompts and facility policy to continue therapy. Confirm return pressure stabilizes and the Return Disconnection alarm does not recur.

steps.title: Monitor for Recurrence
steps.instructions: After correction, observe the device during therapy or simulated operation as appropriate. Confirm return pressure remains stable, the alarm does not recur, no visible leak is present, and no additional pressure monitoring abnormality remains.

CCR.Complaint: PrismaFlex reported Alarm: Return Disconnection during CRRT. Return pressure dropped below expected range and staff reported possible return-line pressure loss during treatment.
CCR.Cause: Return line, catheter connection, chamber monitor line, fluid barrier, and tubing path were inspected. Chamber monitor line was found loose and return pressure reading was unstable. No internal device damage was observed during external inspection.
CCR.Resolution: Secured chamber monitor line, verified return-line and catheter connections, inspected fluid barrier and tubing path, and observed stable return pressure with no repeated Return Disconnection alarm. Device returned to service.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: Return Pressure Dropping
description: Troubleshooting Return Pressure Dropping on the Baxter PrismaFlex when return pressure falls below its operating point, suggesting leak, disconnection, obstruction, disposable set issue, or pressure sensing failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is clinically stable and that CRRT therapy is being safely monitored. If there is concern for blood loss, return-line disconnection, catheter issue, or unsafe treatment condition, stop troubleshooting on the active patient and follow facility protocol.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the PrismaFlex displays Alarm: Return Pressure Dropping. Observe when the alarm occurs and document return pressure trend, blood flow rate, alarm recurrence, visible leaks, loose connections, or blood-flow interruption.

steps.title: Check the Return Line and Catheter Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the return line from the disposable set to the patient access. Confirm the line and catheter are secure, no luer connections are loose, and no visible leakage or pulled tubing is present.

steps.title: Assess for Patient Movement or Catheter Position Effects
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff whether the alarm appeared during patient movement, repositioning, coughing, suctioning, bed movement, or catheter manipulation. Observe whether return pressure stabilizes once patient and access position are stable.

steps.title: Inspect for Obstruction or Leakage Before the Deaeration Chamber
steps.instructions: Check the blood flowpath before the deaeration chamber for kinked tubing, clamped tubing, restricted tubing, loose connections, leakage, or improperly seated disposable set components.

steps.title: Check the Chamber Monitor Line and Fluid Barrier
steps.instructions: Inspect the chamber monitor line and hydrophobic membrane or fluid barrier. Confirm the monitor line is connected, not kinked, and the fluid barrier is dry. If wet, stop therapy and change the set according to facility procedure.

steps.title: Check for a Disconnected Service Line
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible service-line connections associated with the pressure monitoring path. Confirm no service line is loose, disconnected, damaged, or leaking. Do not perform internal disassembly during bedside troubleshooting.

steps.title: Change the Set if Indicated
steps.instructions: If there is a wet fluid barrier, suspected disposable-set defect, persistent leakage, or unsafe pressure monitoring condition, remove the set from use per facility procedure and load a new set if directed.

steps.title: Remove From Service if the Problem Persists
steps.instructions: If the alarm recurs with a new set and external causes have been ruled out, remove the PrismaFlex from service, label it Out of Service, and send it for bench evaluation of return pressure sensing and ARPS circuit leakage.

CCR.Complaint: PrismaFlex reported Alarm: Return Pressure Dropping during CRRT. Return pressure dropped below operating point and alarm recurred during treatment.
CCR.Cause: Return line, catheter connection, tubing path, chamber monitor line, and fluid barrier were inspected. No external leak or obstruction was found. Alarm recurred with new set, indicating possible return pressure sensing or ARPS circuit issue.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from patient use and labeled Out of Service. Sent for bench evaluation of return pressure sensor accuracy, ARPS circuit leakage, and pressure calibration.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: Set Disconnection
description: Troubleshooting Set Disconnection alarms on the Baxter PrismaFlex caused by low filter pressure, possible set or pod disconnection, obstruction, access flow instability, or pressure sensing failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the PrismaFlex from active patient use if the alarm cannot be quickly verified and corrected. Confirm the patient is clinically stable, involve the bedside clinical team, and follow facility procedure for CRRT interruption or transfer to backup therapy.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the screen displays Alarm: Set Disconnection. Observe whether it occurs during startup, priming, treatment, movement, flow changes, set change, or pressure pod adjustment. Document filter pressure behavior and any associated symptoms.

steps.title: Check the Filter Pressure Pod Installation
steps.instructions: Inspect the filter pressure pod for proper seating in the pressure sensor housing. Reinstall it according to facility procedure if needed and confirm it is fully seated, aligned, and not loose.

steps.title: Inspect the Sensor Housing for Debris or Contamination
steps.instructions: Check the filter pressure sensor housing for debris, dried fluid, residue, or anything preventing proper pod contact. Clean visible external contamination using facility-approved cleaning procedures without internal disassembly.

steps.title: Check the Line Between the Blood Pump and Filter
steps.instructions: Trace the blood flowpath from the blood pump to the filter. Confirm tubing is fully connected, properly routed, not separated, and not pulled loose from the set.

steps.title: Check for Upstream Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect the blood flowpath before the filter for closed clamps, kinks, compressed lines, improper routing, obstruction near the blood pump, or restricted access flow. Correct any external obstruction found.

steps.title: Verify Blood Flow Is Appropriate for the Access Device
steps.instructions: Review the prescribed blood flow rate with clinical staff and confirm it is appropriate for the patient’s access device. Coordinate with clinical staff before any therapy setting changes.

steps.title: Check for Access or Set-Related Causes
steps.instructions: Work with clinical staff to check for poor catheter flow, line reversal, patient movement affecting access flow, clotted or malpositioned catheter, improper set loading, or recent set manipulation.

steps.title: Perform the System Tools Self-Test
steps.instructions: If external tubing, pod, and access checks do not resolve the alarm, perform the appropriate self-test through SYSTEM TOOLS if allowed by facility procedure. Confirm the self-test completes and pressure readings normalize.

steps.title: Replace the Set if the Alarm Remains Set-Related
steps.instructions: If the pod, tubing, and flowpath appear correct but the alarm continues, change the set according to PrismaFlex and facility procedure. Load and prime the new set correctly and monitor for recurrence.

steps.title: Recheck Operation After Corrective Action
steps.instructions: Confirm the alarm clears, filter pressure displays appropriately, no tubing disconnection is present, and no new access, return, filter, or pressure alarms appear.

CCR.Complaint: PrismaFlex reported Alarm: Set Disconnection during CRRT. Filter pressure dropped below +10 mmHg after previously operating above +10 mmHg, causing therapy interruption.
CCR.Cause: Filter pressure pod and blood flowpath inspected. No external tubing disconnection or obstruction found. Alarm persisted after pod reseating and set replacement, indicating likely filter pressure sensing or ARPS-related failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed device from service and labeled Out of Service. Sent for bench evaluation of filter pressure sensor, ARPS circuit leakage, and pressure sensor calibration. Device not returned to service pending repair verification.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: Filter Clotted
description: Troubleshooting guide for the Baxter PrismaFlex Filter Clotted alarm, including external circuit checks, anticoagulation delivery review, ultrafiltration demand assessment, pressure monitoring checks, escalation, and CCR documentation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
