steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is being safely supported before troubleshooting. If therapy is interrupted, pressures are unsafe, blood flow is compromised, or clotting is suspected, involve the bedside clinical team immediately and do not continue relying on a clotted or unsafe circuit.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is Alarm: Filter Clotted. Observe whether it occurs during active treatment, after rate changes, after anticoagulation interruption, repeatedly after reset, or with Filter is Clotting Advisory or TMP Excessive Caution messages.

steps.title: Translate the Alarm Into the Real-World Condition
steps.instructions: Understand that the alarm indicates excessive pressure drop across the filter. Possible causes include clot formation, restricted blood flow, inadequate anticoagulation, excessive ultrafiltration demand, pressure measurement error, or syringe/PBP pump delivery failure.

steps.title: Check Blood-Flow Lines for Clamps or Kinks
steps.instructions: Inspect the blood-flow path for clamped, kinked, compressed, or restricted tubing. Confirm lines are routed correctly and are not pinched by bed rails, pump doors, equipment, or patient positioning.

steps.title: Assess for Visible or Suspected Circuit Clotting
steps.instructions: Inspect the filter and visible circuit areas for darkening, fibrin, sluggish flow, or clot formation. Review filter pressure, return pressure, TMP, access pressure, and prior clotting advisories. If clotting is present, have the clinical team change the set using the appropriate PrismaFlex STOP process.

steps.title: Confirm Anticoagulation Status With Clinical Staff
steps.instructions: Ask whether anticoagulation has been interrupted, reduced, delayed, or changed. Confirm the syringe or citrate/PBP therapy is installed and programmed according to the ordered treatment.

steps.title: Check Syringe Pump or PBP Pump Delivery
steps.instructions: Inspect anticoagulant or citrate delivery for an empty syringe or bag, incorrect syringe installation, occluded line, disconnected anticoagulant line, pump alarms, PBP delivery interruption, incorrect setup, or incorrect flow-rate entry.

steps.title: Evaluate Ultrafiltration and Fluid Removal Demands
steps.instructions: Review whether replacement solution, PBP solution, or patient fluid removal rates were recently increased. If clinically appropriate, have the care team reduce excessive rates.

steps.title: Check for Pressure Measurement Issues
steps.instructions: Inspect pressure pods and monitor lines for improper seating, loose connections, wet or contaminated pressure barriers, debris in sensor areas, or unrealistic pressure readings.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Circuit-Related or Device-Related
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after correcting an external restriction, setup issue, rate issue, or circuit clotting condition, document the finding. If it repeats after a new set and external causes are ruled out, suspect pressure sensing error or syringe/PBP pump malfunction.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: Verify normal operation with the clinical team, confirm pressures are stable, and ensure no related clotting or TMP alarms remain active before returning the PrismaFlex to service.

CCR.Complaint: PrismaFlex removed from service due to repeated Filter Clotted alarm during CRRT treatment. Staff reported excessive filter pressure drop and clotting advisory or TMP caution conditions.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found no external tubing clamp or kink. Alarm recurred after user checks, indicating likely circuit clotting or possible pressure measurement, syringe pump, or PBP pump delivery issue.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from patient use and labeled Out of Service. Recommended bench evaluation of pressure sensing function and syringe/PBP pump operation before return to service.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: Plasmafilter Clotted
description: Troubleshooting guide for the PrismaFlex Plasmafilter Clotted alarm, including circuit restrictions, suspected plasmafilter clotting, anticoagulation or citrate delivery issues, flow settings, and pressure measurement concerns.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the PrismaFlex from active patient treatment if the alarm cannot be cleared safely or if filter clotting is suspected. Do not continue therapy through a clotted or partially clotted plasmafilter. Follow facility policy for returning blood, ending treatment, or changing the set.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is Plasmafilter Clotted. Observe whether the alarm occurs during active treatment, after pressure trends increase, after replacement flow or plasma-loss rate changes, after anticoagulant or citrate delivery changes, or intermittently with patient movement or line restriction.

steps.title: Check for Immediate Line Restrictions
steps.instructions: Inspect the blood-flow path for clamped access or return lines, partially closed clamps, kinked tubing, compressed tubing, improper set routing, or patient positioning causing restriction. Correct visible restrictions and observe pressure behavior.

steps.title: Assess the Plasmafilter and Circuit for Clotting
steps.instructions: Inspect the set and plasmafilter as allowed by facility procedure. Look for darkened filter fibers, visible clots, increasing pressure trends, sluggish blood flow, repeated pressure-drop alarms, or recent history of inadequate anticoagulation. If clotting is present or strongly suspected, change the set through STOP according to clinical protocol.

steps.title: Review Anticoagulation Status With Clinical Staff
steps.instructions: Coordinate with clinical staff to review anticoagulation orders, recent anticoagulation changes, ACT, aPTT, ionized calcium, facility-specific clotting values, citrate protocol status, and evidence of frequent circuit clotting.

steps.title: Check Anticoagulant or Citrate Delivery
steps.instructions: Verify syringe pump operation, syringe installation, syringe size and seating, PBP pump operation if citrate is delivered through PBP, pump door closure, tubing placement through the pump segment, and any related syringe pump, PBP, or flow alarms.

steps.title: Review Replacement Flow and Plasma-Loss Rate
steps.instructions: Check whether replacement flow or patient plasma-loss rate was recently increased or is excessive for the treatment setup. Work with clinical staff to adjust settings if clinically appropriate.

steps.title: Check Pressure Measurement Reliability
steps.instructions: If the alarm occurs without visible clotting, line restriction, or clinical explanation, check pressure pod seating, tubing placement, moisture, debris, damage near pressure-sensing areas, loose pressure components, or repeated pressure-related alarms across different sets.

steps.title: Isolate Accessory, Clinical, or Device Cause
steps.instructions: Use findings to narrow the cause. Line correction suggests restriction. Visible clotting suggests set or plasmafilter clotting. Repeated clotting across sets suggests anticoagulation, citrate delivery, or therapy setting concerns. Failed delivery pumps or abnormal pressure readings without a circuit cause require service evaluation.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after correcting a clamp, kink, tubing restriction, flow setting, or pump loading issue, monitor for stable pressures and document the correction and final device status.

CCR.Complaint: PrismaFlex reported Alarm: Plasmafilter Clotted during treatment. Staff reported increasing filter pressure drop and therapy interruption.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found restricted blood-flow tubing from a partially closed clamp, causing elevated pressure drop across the plasmafilter. No device hardware failure confirmed.
CCR.Resolution: Corrected tubing restriction, verified clamps and line routing, confirmed pressure readings stabilized, and returned device to service after clinical staff verified treatment status.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: HP Cartridge Clotted
description: Troubleshooting guide for Baxter PrismaFlex HP Cartridge Clotted alarms caused by cartridge pressure drop, clotting, line restriction, anticoagulation-delivery issues, or pressure measurement failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the PrismaFlex is actively connected to a patient, involve the clinical care team immediately. Do not continue treatment on a CRRT system with unresolved clotting alarms unless the care team determines it is safe to do so.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is HP Cartridge Clotted. Document when the alarm occurs, whether it repeats after reset, and whether cartridge pressure drop, visible clotting, or related pressure alarms are present.

steps.title: Inspect the Blood-Flow Lines for Simple Restrictions
steps.instructions: Check for clamped lines, kinked tubing, pinched tubing, tubing caught in equipment, or excessive routing tension. Correct any external restriction found and observe whether the alarm clears.

steps.title: Check for Visible Cartridge or Circuit Clotting
steps.instructions: With the clinical team, visually inspect the HP cartridge and accessible circuit areas for darkened blood path, fibrin buildup, clot formation, uneven flow, or pressure trends suggesting restriction.

steps.title: Review Anticoagulation Status With Clinical Staff
steps.instructions: Ask the care team to confirm whether anticoagulation is ordered, active, and appropriate. Check for empty syringe, incorrect syringe installation, paused pump, occluded line, incorrect settings, or patient clotting concerns.

steps.title: Confirm the Anticoagulant Delivery Path
steps.instructions: Externally inspect syringe seating, plunger engagement, tubing connection, clamps, kinks, leaks, and syringe pump alarms or unusual syringe pump behavior.

steps.title: Evaluate Blood Flow Setting With the Care Team
steps.instructions: Review whether blood flow is appropriate for the access, cartridge, and patient condition. Clinical staff may adjust therapy settings according to hospital protocol if appropriate.

steps.title: Do Not Continue With a Suspected Clotted Cartridge
steps.instructions: If clotting has occurred or the alarm remains after correcting line restrictions, do not rely on the affected set for continued therapy. Follow PrismaFlex operating procedure and hospital policy for stopping treatment and changing the set.

steps.title: Perform Device Self-Test if Pressure Measurement Failure Is Suspected
steps.instructions: If the alarm occurs without visible clotting, line restriction, or anticoagulation issue, remove the device from active patient use when clinically safe and perform required self-test or bench evaluation.

steps.title: Check Syringe Pump Function if Anticoagulant Delivery Failed
steps.instructions: During bench testing, evaluate syringe pump movement, syringe advancement, mechanical binding, syringe detection, repeated syringe alarms, and diagnostic test results.

steps.title: Isolate the Likely Cause
steps.instructions: Determine whether the alarm was caused by external line restriction, cartridge clotting, anticoagulant delivery interruption, pressure measurement failure, or repeated device-related failure after proper setup.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears and pressures stabilize, document the finding. Return the device to service only when the clinical team confirms safe therapy continuation and no device failure is suspected.

CCR.Complaint: PrismaFlex reported Alarm: HP Cartridge Clotted during CRRT treatment. Staff reported cartridge pressure drop alarm and concern for possible hemopurification cartridge clotting.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found restricted blood-flow tubing at the cartridge inlet. Line was corrected and alarm cleared. No device pressure measurement failure was identified during observation.
CCR.Resolution: Unclamped and rerouted restricted blood-flow line. Verified HP Cartridge Clotted alarm cleared and pressures stabilized. Device remained in service after clinical team confirmed treatment could continue.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: Blood Leak Detected
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Baxter PrismaFlex Blood Leak Detected alarm caused by possible blood in effluent, tubing-position issues, air bubbles, detector contamination, discolored plasma, filter membrane leakage, or blood leak detector failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Respond immediately, notify clinical staff, and do not ignore or bypass the Blood Leak Detected alarm. If the patient is actively connected, coordinate with nursing and follow facility CRRT escalation procedures before handling the set, effluent line, detector area, or blood return process.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is Blood Leak Detected. Document when it occurs, whether it repeats after normalization or reinsertion, and whether the effluent appears clear, pink, red, cloudy, discolored, or normal.

steps.title: Inspect the Effluent Line at the Blood Leak Detector
steps.instructions: Inspect the effluent line where it passes through the detector. Check for bubbles, incorrect routing, tubing not fully seated, twisted or pinched tubing, residue, moisture, or visible obstruction.

steps.title: Check for Air Bubbles at the Detector
steps.instructions: If a bubble is visible at or near the detector, work with clinical staff to clear it according to facility procedure. Do not aggressively manipulate the line while the patient is connected.

steps.title: Check for Moisture, Liquid, or Debris in the Detector Area
steps.instructions: Inspect for effluent drips, cleaning residue, dried fluid, dust, debris, wet tubing exterior, or cracked tubing. Clean and dry the external detector area using approved practices without forcing liquid into the detector.

steps.title: Reinsert the Line and Normalize the Blood Leak Detector if Appropriate
steps.instructions: After correcting tubing placement, bubbles, or contamination, reinsert the effluent line correctly. Normalize the blood leak detector after reinsertion only when clinically appropriate and consistent with procedure and policy.

steps.title: Evaluate for True Filter Membrane Leakage
steps.instructions: If the alarm persists or effluent appears blood-tinged, treat it as possible filter membrane leakage. Notify clinical staff, follow facility protocol for stopping therapy and changing the set, and send an effluent sample for cell count when required.

steps.title: Consider Therapy-Specific False Triggers
steps.instructions: For TPE or plasma-related therapies, verify whether plasma or effluent discoloration may be affecting detector readings while still treating the alarm as a patient-safety concern.

steps.title: Test With a New Set if Clinically Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the set is changed due to suspected leakage or repeated alarms, monitor whether the Blood Leak Detected alarm recurs with the new set.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Problem Is Accessory, Set, or Device Related
steps.instructions: Use findings to isolate the issue. Alarm clearing after bubble removal, tubing reinsertion, cleaning, or set replacement points to an external, set, or fluid-path cause. Recurrence with a new set and clean detector path may indicate blood leak detector failure.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: When the alarm clears, verify stable operation, document the corrective action, final alarm status, and whether the device remained in service or was removed for repair.

CCR.Complaint: PrismaFlex reported Blood Leak Detected alarm during treatment. Staff reported alarm at the effluent line/blood leak detector area with concern for possible blood in the effluent path.
CCR.Cause: Effluent line inspected for blood leak detector false-trigger causes. Tubing position, air bubble at detector, detector-path contamination, moisture, and set/filter condition evaluated. Alarm suspected to be caused by incorrect effluent-line seating and air bubble at detector.
CCR.Resolution: Removed device from patient dependency during evaluation. Reinstalled effluent line correctly, cleared visible bubble from detector area with clinical staff, verified detector path was clean and dry, normalized detector after reinsertion per procedure, and confirmed alarm cleared without recurrence. Device returned to service after successful functional verification.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: Access Extremely Negative
description: Troubleshooting guide for the PrismaFlex Access Extremely Negative alarm caused by excessive negative access pressure, access-line restriction, catheter issues, blood flow demand, disposable set problems, or pressure-sensing failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is being safely supported. Do not continue troubleshooting on an active treatment if blood flow is compromised or the alarm cannot be resolved quickly. Notify clinical staff and follow facility protocol for CRRT interruption, blood return, anticoagulation concerns, and access catheter management.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact displayed alarm is Access Extremely Negative. Observe whether the alarm occurs during startup, shortly after treatment begins, after patient movement, during coughing or suctioning, during transport, or only at higher blood flow rates. Check for blood pump interruption and access pressure trending more negative.

steps.title: Allow the Self-Clearing Attempt When Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the patient is stable and no unsafe condition is visible, allow the PrismaFlex to complete the short self-clearing attempt. If the alarm clears and does not return, continue monitoring. If the alarm repeats, continue with external checks.

steps.title: Check the Access Line for External Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect the access line from the patient catheter to the PrismaFlex set. Check for closed clamps, kinks, twisted tubing, trapped tubing, line tension, or tubing collapse during pump operation. Correct any visible obstruction.

steps.title: Check for Patient Movement or Catheter Position Effects
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff whether the alarm started after repositioning, coughing, suctioning, transport, line manipulation, or dressing changes. With clinical staff, verify the catheter is not being pulled, bent, compressed, or positioned against the vessel wall.

steps.title: Have Clinical Staff Evaluate the Access Catheter
steps.instructions: Ask qualified clinical staff to assess the access catheter according to hospital protocol. Clinical checks may include blood withdrawal assessment, flushing per protocol, patient repositioning, catheter repositioning, dressing assessment, or evaluation for clotting or malposition.

steps.title: Compare Blood Flow Rate to Access Capability
steps.instructions: Review the prescribed blood flow rate and access device. If the alarm occurs mostly at higher blood flow rates, ask clinical staff to reduce blood flow only according to protocol and provider direction. Document whether lower flow stabilizes the alarm.

steps.title: Inspect the Pressure Pod and Set Interface
steps.instructions: Check that the access pressure pod is correctly seated. Verify nearby tubing is fully positioned in the pressure-sensing area. Look for moisture, leakage, contamination, damaged tubing, or incorrect disposable set loading.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Access-Related or Device-Related
steps.instructions: If the alarm follows patient movement, catheter function, catheter position, or blood flow rate, suspect an access-related cause. If the alarm occurs with no obstruction, stable catheter function, correct set loading, and normal operating conditions, suspect a device pressure-sensing issue.

steps.title: Escalate for Pressure-Sensing Evaluation if Needed
steps.instructions: If the alarm persists after line, catheter, flow, pressure pod, and setup checks are complete, remove the PrismaFlex from use, label it Out of Service, and send it for bench evaluation of access pressure sensor operation, ARPS circuit leakage, pressure-sensor calibration, and pressure reading stability.

CCR.Complaint: PrismaFlex removed from CRRT use due to Access Extremely Negative alarm. Staff reported alarm during treatment with access pressure becoming excessively negative and blood pump interruption.
CCR.Cause: Access line and external routing were inspected. No clamp or kink found. Alarm persisted after patient repositioning and blood flow reduction. Catheter issue ruled out by clinical staff. Suspect access pressure-sensing or ARPS circuit fault.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service and labeled Out of Service. Sent for bench evaluation of access pressure sensor, ARPS circuit leakage, and pressure-sensor calibration. Repair required before return to patient use.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: Return Extremely Positive
description: Troubleshooting guide for Baxter PrismaFlex Return Extremely Positive alarms caused by high return pressure, return-line obstruction, catheter restriction, excessive blood flow, set issues, or return pressure-sensing failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is being safely supported during the CRRT alarm condition. Notify the clinical team if therapy is interrupted or return pressure remains elevated. Follow facility policy for monitoring, escalation, blood return, treatment interruption, or transfer to another CRRT device.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the PrismaFlex is displaying Alarm: Return Extremely Positive. Observe when the alarm occurs, whether it is linked to patient movement or repositioning, and whether it self-clears or repeatedly returns.

steps.title: Allow Self-Clearing When Clinically Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the patient is stable and the alarm appears related to a brief event such as coughing, suctioning, movement, repositioning, or temporary catheter pressure change, allow the machine’s self-clearing attempt when appropriate.

steps.title: Inspect the Return Line for External Obstruction
steps.instructions: Trace the return line from the PrismaFlex set to the patient connection. Check for closed clamps, partially closed clamps, kinked tubing, pinched tubing, sharp bends, or line tension. Correct visible restrictions and verify return pressure stabilizes.

steps.title: Check Catheter Position and Patient-Related Causes
steps.instructions: Coordinate with the clinical team to assess whether the return catheter may be restricted, clotted, malpositioned, or affected by patient position. Clinical staff should manage catheter flushing, repositioning, or access evaluation according to hospital protocol.

steps.title: Review Blood Flow Settings
steps.instructions: Check whether the blood flow rate is set higher than the catheter or access can tolerate. If clinically appropriate, have the care team reduce blood flow and observe whether return pressure decreases.

steps.title: Use RELEASE CLAMP if Available and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the PrismaFlex provides a RELEASE CLAMP option for this condition, follow facility procedure and manufacturer guidance to relieve excess return-line pressure when appropriate. Do not repeatedly release pressure without identifying the cause.

steps.title: Check for Accessory or Set-Related Issues
steps.instructions: Inspect the loaded PrismaFlex set for an improperly seated return pressure pod, wet or damaged pressure pod interface, incorrect tubing loading, tubing tension, visible clotting, set damage, or abnormal tubing collapse.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Patient, Circuit, or Device Related
steps.instructions: Use the troubleshooting response to narrow the source. Movement-related alarms suggest transient patient or access pressure changes. Improvement after removing a kink suggests line obstruction. Improvement after catheter adjustment suggests access restriction. Persistent alarms after external causes are ruled out suggest return pressure sensing or internal device failure.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after correcting a line, catheter, flow, or setup issue, confirm stable return pressure, verify no related alarms are active, confirm normal circuit operation, return the device to service if clinically appropriate, and document the cause and resolution.

CCR.Complaint: PrismaFlex reported Return Extremely Positive alarm during treatment. Return pressure exceeded the user-set warning limit and the alarm recurred after an initial self-clear attempt.
CCR.Cause: Return line was found kinked near the patient connection, causing excessive return pressure. Pressure stabilized after tubing was rerouted.
CCR.Resolution: Corrected return-line routing, verified clamps were open, observed stable return pressure, confirmed no related alarms were active, and returned device to service.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: Filter Extremely Positive
description: Troubleshooting guide for the Baxter PrismaFlex Filter Extremely Positive alarm caused by excessive filter pressure, restricted blood flow, clotting, catheter issues, high return pressure, or pressure-sensing failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is stable and clinical staff are aware of the alarm. Do not interrupt therapy or manipulate the circuit without nursing or provider involvement. If clotting, excessive pressure, or unsafe blood return is suspected, follow facility CRRT policy and clinical direction.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is Filter Extremely Positive. Check whether filter pressure is at or above 450 mmHg, whether return pressure is elevated, whether blood flow appears restricted, and whether the circuit shows clotting, darkening, poor flow, or repeated pressure alarms.

steps.title: Translate the Alarm Into the Real-World Condition
steps.instructions: Treat the alarm as excessive pressure at the filter pressure pod. Focus troubleshooting on tubing restriction, filter clotting, high return pressure, catheter performance, circuit routing, and pressure-sensing accuracy.

steps.title: Check for Clamped or Kinked Lines
steps.instructions: Inspect the blood-flow path between the filter pressure pod and filter, and between the filter and deaeration chamber. Correct closed clamps, partially closed clamps, kinked tubing, trapped tubing, twisted tubing, or compression from nearby equipment.

steps.title: Confirm Circuit Routing and Set Position
steps.instructions: Verify the disposable set is seated correctly. Check that tubing is not pulled tight, misrouted, or pinched around the cassette, filter, pump area, or pressure pod. Confirm the filter pressure pod is properly positioned and not distorted.

steps.title: Check for High Return Pressure
steps.instructions: Review the return pressure reading and related return-side alarms. Coordinate with clinical staff to assess return line restriction, catheter issues, patient position, catheter patency, or excessive blood flow rate for the access condition.

steps.title: Lower Blood Flow Rate if Clinically Appropriate
steps.instructions: If blood flow is high or return pressure is elevated, clinical staff may lower the blood flow rate according to facility policy and provider direction. Clinical Engineering should not independently change therapy settings unless permitted by local policy.

steps.title: Inspect for Developing Filter Clotting
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff about filter performance and look for dark streaking, visible clot formation, rising TMP trends, repeated pressure alarms, poor flow, or rising filter pressure over time.

steps.title: Relieve Excess Pressure if the System Allows
steps.instructions: If the PrismaFlex displays an available option such as RELEASE CLAMP, have the operator follow the on-screen prompt and facility procedure. Observe whether pressure decreases and the alarm clears.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after correcting a clamp, kink, tubing restriction, catheter issue, or pressure condition, confirm pressures remain stable, no related alarms return, blood flow appears appropriate, and the correction is documented.

steps.title: Change the Set if Operator Correction Does Not Clear the Alarm
steps.instructions: If the alarm does not clear, returns immediately, or the filter appears clotted, the set should be changed using STOP according to operator procedure and facility policy. Clinical staff should determine whether blood can be safely returned.

steps.title: Evaluate for Repeated Alarms After Circuit Correction
steps.instructions: If alarms repeat despite proper tubing, catheter function, flow settings, and a new set, evaluate the machine side. Check the filter pressure pod interface, pressure sensor area, pressure pod seating, ARPS circuit condition, and pressure reading accuracy.

steps.title: Perform Bench Evaluation if Device Failure Is Suspected
steps.instructions: Remove the PrismaFlex from clinical use if repeated alarms suggest pressure-sensing failure. During bench evaluation, check the filter pressure sensor pathway and ARPS-related function, calibrate if indicated, and replace failed sensing components if readings remain inaccurate.

CCR.Complaint: PrismaFlex reported Filter Extremely Positive alarm during CRRT. Filter pressure reached or exceeded 450 mmHg. Staff reported repeated pressure alarms and concern for restricted blood flow or filter clotting.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found kinked tubing between the filter pressure pod and filter, causing elevated filter pressure. Alarm cleared after tubing was rerouted and pressure readings stabilized.
CCR.Resolution: Corrected tubing route, verified no clamps or kinks remained, confirmed filter pressure returned to acceptable range, and monitored for recurring alarms. Device remained in service after clinical staff confirmed therapy could continue.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: General System Failure
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Baxter PrismaFlex General System Failure alarm with Error Code 1 through 6 involving possible pump, clamp, syringe, startup, or communication-related treatment interruption.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the PrismaFlex from patient treatment use when a General System Failure alarm is active. Notify the clinical team immediately and follow facility procedure for patient circuit management and blood return when applicable.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is General System Failure. Document Error Code 1 through 6 if shown and record when the alarm occurred, including startup, treatment, set loading, pump operation, syringe pump use, or after power-up.

steps.title: Follow Immediate Clinical Safety Actions
steps.instructions: Power the machine down if directed by clinical procedure. Remove the return line from the return-line clamp as instructed and return blood only when clinically appropriate.

steps.title: Remove the Device From Service
steps.instructions: After the patient is safely managed, remove the PrismaFlex from clinical use and label it Out of Service — General System Failure Alarm. Include the observed error code if known.

steps.title: Check External and Simple Causes First
steps.instructions: Inspect the power connection, power cord, and outlet. Confirm the device was not connected to an unstable outlet, loose plug, damaged cord, or interrupted power source.

steps.title: Inspect the Loaded Set and External Tubing Path
steps.instructions: Inspect for misloaded tubing, tight pump segments, kinked lines, tubing trapped near the return clamp, improper line seating, visible set damage, or fluid contamination near pump or clamp areas.

steps.title: Inspect the Return-Line Clamp Area
steps.instructions: Check the return-line clamp for obstruction, damage, contamination, or tubing caught in the clamp path. Do not force the clamp mechanism.

steps.title: Inspect Blood Pump and Fluid Pump Areas
steps.instructions: Externally inspect pump raceways for tubing misalignment, pump segment binding, visible damage, fluid residue, pump rotor obstruction, unusual resistance, clicking, grinding, or abnormal startup sounds.

steps.title: Check for Syringe Pump Involvement
steps.instructions: If the alarm occurred during anticoagulation setup or syringe pump operation, inspect the syringe pump area for improper syringe installation, plunger misalignment, carriage obstruction, damaged holder, unexpected movement, or contamination.

steps.title: Power Cycle for Observation Only
steps.instructions: With the device off patient use, power the unit on for bench observation if appropriate. Do not reload a returned treatment set for clinical use. Observe whether the alarm returns at startup, self-test, pump initialization, clamp actuation, or syringe movement.

steps.title: Use the Error Code to Narrow the Failure Area
steps.instructions: Record the specific error code and use it to guide service evaluation toward pump movement, fluid pump movement, return clamp position, Hall sensor feedback, I²C or startup communication, syringe pump movement, or software/control communication issues.

steps.title: Do Not Return to Service Based Only on Alarm Clearing
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after power cycling but occurred during treatment, do not immediately return the device to clinical service. Perform bench evaluation and functional verification according to facility procedure.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If an external cause is found, correct it, clean or remove obstruction as appropriate, verify startup and functional checks, document the final status, and return the device to service only after full verification.

CCR.Complaint: PrismaFlex removed from CRRT use due to General System Failure alarm with Error Code 1–6 reported. Treatment stopped, and alarm indicated possible pump, clamp, syringe, or communication-related failure.
CCR.Cause: Initial inspection found no external tubing obstruction, unstable power source, or visible clamp/pump loading issue. Alarm behavior indicates likely internal system failure involving pump movement detection, return clamp position feedback, syringe pump movement, or startup/I²C communication path.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service and labeled Out of Service. Return-line clamp area, pump raceways, syringe pump area, power connection, and loaded set path inspected. Alarm/error code documented. Unit sent for service evaluation and repair before return to CRRT use.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: Communication Error
description: Troubleshooting guide for Baxter PrismaFlex Communication Error alarms involving Error Codes 2 through 7, including patient safety actions, external checks, restart verification, and escalation for internal communication or software-related failures.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Notify the clinical team, follow facility CRRT downtime or therapy interruption procedure, and do not continue relying on the PrismaFlex if protective/control communication is not functioning correctly.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Error Code
steps.instructions: Confirm the alarm is Communication Error and document the associated Error Code 2 through 7, including when it occurred and whether treatment was active.

steps.title: Understand the Alarm Condition
steps.instructions: Treat the alarm as an internal protective/control communication fault involving the protective task, protective slave, or control-system communication path.

steps.title: Follow the Immediate Clinical Response
steps.instructions: Follow on-screen and facility-approved clinical response, including turning off the machine, removing the return line from the clamp, returning blood when applicable, restarting, and reporting the failure code if the alarm recurs.

steps.title: Remove the Device From Patient Use
steps.instructions: Remove the PrismaFlex from clinical use, label it for Clinical Engineering evaluation, and retain the reported alarm code and staff observations.

steps.title: Check External Power and Basic Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify AC outlet power, power cord condition, plug condition, battery or power-related messages, and normal startup behavior after restart.

steps.title: Inspect External Cables, Doors, and Visible Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect for fluid intrusion, damage, loose external connections, impact, unusual heat or odor, fan noise, display issues, keypad issues, and recent service tags.

steps.title: Restart and Attempt to Reproduce the Alarm
steps.instructions: Restart the unit off-patient and document whether the Communication Error returns at startup, during self-test, during simulated setup, after runtime, or not at all.

steps.title: Review Error History and Service Pattern
steps.instructions: Compare the code with recent service history and look for repeated codes, multiple communication-related alarms, recent software service, recent board replacement, fluid events, or treatment interruptions.

steps.title: Escalate to Qualified Service Evaluation
steps.instructions: If the alarm recurs, escalate for approved service evaluation of software download concerns, I²C bus communication, internal connections, board voltage, and failed electronic components.

steps.title: Verify Before Return to Service
steps.instructions: Before return to service, confirm normal startup, no Communication Error recurrence, no related alarms, normal controls, proper display and alarm behavior, and completion of required facility checks.

CCR.Complaint: PrismaFlex displayed Communication Error alarm with Error Code 2 to 7 during startup or treatment. Staff reported therapy interruption and unit required restart.
CCR.Cause: Communication fault confirmed or reported between protective task, protective slave, or control-system communication path. External power and visible condition checked. Recurring alarm indicates likely internal communication, software, board voltage, or component failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed PrismaFlex from service for bench evaluation. Restart behavior and alarm code documented. If alarm did not recur, operational verification was completed before return to service. If alarm recurred, unit remained Out of Service for qualified repair.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: Memory Error
description: Troubleshooting guide for Baxter PrismaFlex Memory Error alarms involving treatment interruption, flow-setting mismatch, CRC failure, protective/control system disagreement, or backup-memory failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Treat a Memory Error alarm during treatment as a patient-safety issue. Notify clinical staff, confirm whether therapy must be stopped or transferred to another CRRT device, and follow facility procedure for blood return when applicable.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Error Code
steps.instructions: Confirm the alarm is Memory Error and document the associated error code if shown. Record whether the alarm occurred during startup, setup, flow-setting entry, syringe installation, active therapy, or restart.

steps.title: Stop Therapy Safely if Treatment Is Active
steps.instructions: Coordinate with clinical staff before touching the circuit. Turn off the machine only when clinically appropriate, and remove the return line from the return-line clamp and return blood when applicable according to procedure.

steps.title: Restart the PrismaFlex Control Unit
steps.instructions: After patient and circuit safety are addressed, power the PrismaFlex off, wait briefly, and restart the control unit. Observe whether the Memory Error returns.

steps.title: Check for Flow-Rate Discrepancy if Error Code 6 Is Present
steps.instructions: Ask whether flow rates differed between the Status screen and Enter Flow Settings screen. Restart the control unit, reinstall the syringe if needed, re-enter prescribed fluid flows, and confirm displayed flow values match.

steps.title: Check Setup and External Factors Before Assuming Internal Failure
steps.instructions: Inspect for incomplete syringe installation, interrupted setup sequence, recently changed flow settings, power interruption, screen mismatch, repeated alarm after restart, control-unit damage, or unusual sounds, heat, smell, or display behavior.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Alarm Is Code-Specific
steps.instructions: For Error Codes 1, 3, 4, 5, or 7, treat the alarm as a likely software memory integrity or system verification issue and do not return the device to use if the alarm recurs.

steps.title: Verify Whether the Alarm Recurs Off-Patient
steps.instructions: Attempt to reproduce the issue only while the device is off-patient. Confirm whether the alarm clears, returns on startup, returns during flow-setting entry, returns during syringe setup, or returns during simulated setup or priming.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: Return the device to service only if the Memory Error does not recur, the error code was documented, flow settings display correctly, no other alarms are active, and clinical staff confirm setup values before use.

CCR.Complaint: PrismaFlex reported Memory Error alarm with Error Code 6 during treatment setup. Staff reported flow values did not match between the Status screen and Enter Flow Settings screen.
CCR.Cause: Flow-rate display discrepancy suspected. External setup issue checked first; syringe installation and entered fluid flows required re-verification. Memory Error did not recur after restart and flow re-entry.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from patient use for evaluation. Restarted control unit, reinstalled syringe as needed, re-entered fluid flows, and confirmed displayed flow settings matched. No Memory Error alarm returned during verification. Device returned to service.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: Pressures Circuit Board
description: Troubleshooting guide for the Baxter PrismaFlex Pressures Circuit Board alarm when pressure-sensing hardware failure may affect CRRT pressure monitoring and safe device operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Treat the alarm as a patient-safety condition. Stop use according to clinical procedure, notify the care team, support the patient with an alternate device or therapy plan if CRRT must continue, and follow displayed instructions for shutdown, return-line clamp handling, and blood return when clinically appropriate.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact displayed alarm is Pressures Circuit Board. Document whether it occurred at startup, setup, priming, active therapy, or intermittently after movement or pressure pod repositioning. Note any related access, return, filter, effluent, or pressure pod alarms.

steps.title: Remove the Device From Active Clinical Use
steps.instructions: Confirm treatment was stopped safely and blood return was handled according to facility procedure if applicable. Do not continue therapy on the affected PrismaFlex. Mark the device for Clinical Engineering evaluation.

steps.title: Check External and Simple Causes First
steps.instructions: Inspect pressure pods and pressure pod connection areas. Confirm pods are properly seated, not cracked, not contaminated, and not visibly damaged. Verify circuit installation and clean any residue, moisture, or debris from pressure pod areas.

steps.title: Inspect the Exterior Condition of the Device
steps.instructions: Inspect the front panel, pressure pod interface area, clamps, display, and cartridge loading area for fluid intrusion, cleaning residue, cracked plastic, impact damage, corrosion, or contamination. Escalate immediately if damage or fluid intrusion is found.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: With the device removed from patient use, power down and restart the PrismaFlex only if allowed by facility procedure. Observe whether the alarm clears, returns at startup, or returns during setup or pressure pod checks.

steps.title: Check Pressure Sensor Function in Diagnostics
steps.instructions: Use the Diagnose Screen – Pressure Pod Reposition check to evaluate pressure sensor function. Observe whether sensors respond normally and whether pressure pod repositioning is recognized correctly. Record abnormal, unstable, missing, or failed sensor responses.

steps.title: Isolate the Likely Failure
steps.instructions: If pressure pods, circuit setup, exterior condition, and restart checks do not resolve the alarm, treat the issue as device-related. The likely failure area is the pressure-sensing hardware path, especially the pressures circuit board.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after correcting pod seating, contamination, setup, or restart-related conditions, complete full operational verification before return to service. Confirm pressure readings behave normally and the alarm does not return during setup checks.

CCR.Complaint: PrismaFlex reported Alarm: Pressures Circuit Board during CRRT setup. Staff reported the machine instructed shutdown and service call due to pressure circuit board hardware failure.
CCR.Cause: Pressure pods and external setup were inspected. Alarm persisted after basic checks. Diagnostic pressure sensor evaluation indicated likely malfunction in the pressure-sensing hardware path.
CCR.Resolution: Removed PrismaFlex from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation. Pressures circuit board replacement required if malfunction is confirmed. Device not returned to service pending repair and verification.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: Voltage Out of Range
description: Troubleshooting guide for the Baxter PrismaFlex Voltage Out of Range alarm, which indicates a possible power-supply, internal power-cabling, control-unit, or related pump/control malfunction affecting safe CRRT operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not continue CRRT therapy on a PrismaFlex displaying Voltage Out of Range. Notify clinical staff immediately and follow facility procedure for therapy interruption, blood return, and transition to another CRRT machine if therapy must continue.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is Alarm: Voltage Out of Range. Document whether the alarm occurs at startup, during setup, during active therapy, after movement, or intermittently. Note any therapy interruption, pump stop, display instability, restart failure, heat, odor, noise, or visible damage.

steps.title: Remove the Device From Active Use
steps.instructions: Remove the PrismaFlex from clinical use once the alarm is confirmed. Do not return the device to service without evaluation if the alarm returns, occurs during therapy, or cannot be explained by an external power issue.

steps.title: Check External Power First
steps.instructions: Verify the AC outlet and power cord before assuming internal failure. Confirm the cord is fully seated, inspect for damage or overheating, and test the device on a known-good hospital-grade outlet without power strips.

steps.title: Check for Movement-Related or Connection-Related Failure
steps.instructions: Ask whether the alarm occurred after the device was moved, bumped, unplugged, repositioned, or transferred. Inspect accessible external connections and the power cord strain relief area for looseness, damage, overheating, or fluid intrusion.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only for Verification
steps.instructions: With the unit removed from patient use, restart it only as part of controlled troubleshooting. Confirm whether the Voltage Out of Range alarm returns at startup or during self-test.

steps.title: Check for Other Related Symptoms
steps.instructions: Observe for unexpected shutdown, display flicker, pump interruption, repeated startup failure, alarm recurrence under load, abnormal fan or pump noise, abnormal power-supply noise, unusual heat, or odor.

steps.title: Isolate From Accessory or Environmental Causes
steps.instructions: Confirm the condition is not related to the treatment set, scale interference, room outlet, or external accessories. Use a known-good outlet and remove nonessential external accessories during bench evaluation.

steps.title: Perform Service-Level Power Supply Evaluation
steps.instructions: If the alarm persists, perform the PrismaFlex power supply check according to service procedure. Verify power supply output values are within specification and inspect power-supply cabling and connectors for loose seating, damage, overheating, or poor contact.

steps.title: Check Blood Pump Function if Directed by Service Procedure
steps.instructions: Use Diagnose Screen – Pumps Diagnose when directed by service procedure. Verify blood pump operation and check for abnormal behavior, failure to run, irregular motion, or diagnostic failure.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: After correcting the issue, perform full functional verification. Confirm the alarm does not return, startup and self-test complete normally, pumps operate properly, no abnormal noise, heat, smell, or display instability is present, and final device status is documented.

CCR.Complaint: PrismaFlex removed from CRRT use due to Voltage Out of Range alarm. Staff reported alarm during treatment/setup with therapy interruption and concern for internal power malfunction.
CCR.Cause: External power source and power cord checked. Alarm persisted on known-good outlet. Condition indicates likely internal power-supply, power-supply cabling, control-unit, or related blood pump malfunction.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service and labeled Out of Service. Performed/initiated power supply check and pump diagnostic evaluation. Unit referred for repair with power supply and blood pump replacement to be performed if diagnostic values or pump operation are out of specification.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: Scale Open
description: Troubleshooting guide for the Baxter PrismaFlex Scale Open alarm during priming when a scale cannot fully close or the machine cannot detect closure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the PrismaFlex is not actively supporting a patient. Do not proceed to patient connection until the Scale Open alarm clears. If therapy is urgently needed, provide clinical staff with another ready CRRT system according to facility procedure.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is Alarm: Scale Open. Verify it occurs during priming and identify the affected scale if indicated. Observe whether the scale remains open, appears closed but still alarms, or returns after pressing CONTINUE.

steps.title: Check for Obstruction Around the Scale
steps.instructions: Inspect for tubing, bag edges, packaging, caps, clamps, loose accessories, contamination, or visible damage preventing full closure. Remove obstructions and gently push the scale closed until it locks.

steps.title: Verify Bag Positioning
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct bag is on the correct scale, hanging freely, not twisted or wedged, and not pulling the scale off-center. Reposition the bag and press CONTINUE.

steps.title: Check the Carrying Bar and Bar Tray
steps.instructions: Verify the carrying bar is centered, the bar tray is seated, and the bar is not angled, bent, loose, obstructed, or pulled sideways by the bag.

steps.title: Confirm Carrying-Bar Handle Orientation
steps.instructions: Verify the carrying-bar handle is rotated down toward the floor. Rotate it fully downward if needed, then close the scale again.

steps.title: Confirm the Scale Locks Closed
steps.instructions: Push the scale toward the PrismaFlex using steady pressure until it locks closed. Do not slam the scale. Press CONTINUE when prompted.

steps.title: Isolate Whether the Issue Is Mechanical or Sensor-Related
steps.instructions: If the scale physically closes but the alarm remains, check for loose latching, spring-back, blocked closure points, continued Scale Open display, and whether another scale closes normally.

steps.title: Check Scale Sensor Status in Diagnostics
steps.instructions: Use Diagnose Screen – Scale Diagnose to check whether the affected scale closure sensor changes state when the scale is opened and closed.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If the alarm clears after repositioning the bag, correcting the carrying bar, rotating the handle downward, removing an obstruction, or properly locking the scale, return the PrismaFlex to the priming workflow and document the corrective action.

CCR.Complaint: PrismaFlex reported Alarm: Scale Open during priming. Staff unable to continue setup because the scale would not close or the machine did not detect closure.
CCR.Cause: Scale bag and carrying bar area inspected. Bag position and carrying-bar alignment corrected. Carrying-bar handle was not fully rotated down toward the floor, preventing proper scale closure.
CCR.Resolution: Repositioned bag, centered carrying bar, rotated handle downward, pushed scale closed until locked, and pressed CONTINUE. Alarm cleared and priming resumed normally. Device returned to service.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: Scales
description: Troubleshooting the Baxter PrismaFlex Scales alarm when an identified scale is out of calibration and CRRT fluid-balance accuracy may be affected.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Treat the Scales alarm as a patient-safety issue because PrismaFlex therapy depends on accurate scale measurement. Notify clinical staff, do not continue relying on the machine if the alarm cannot be cleared, and move the patient to a safe clinical plan before further troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is Alarm: Scales. Check the alarm screen to identify the affected scale, document when the alarm occurred, and press RETEST as instructed by the system.

steps.title: Check External and Simple Causes First
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct bag is installed on the correct scale, verify the bag hangs freely, check that tubing is not pulling on the bag or hanger, and inspect the scale hook and surrounding area for obstruction, damage, bending, or contamination.

steps.title: Confirm Alarm Response After External Checks
steps.instructions: After correcting any setup, bag-positioning, tubing-tension, or interference issue, press RETEST again. If the alarm clears, document the correction and monitor for recurrence.

steps.title: If the Alarm Does Not Clear During Treatment
steps.instructions: Follow the PrismaFlex treatment-discontinuation process. Clinical staff should end treatment through DISCONNECT as applicable, turn the machine off when directed, remove the return line from the clamp, and return blood when applicable.

steps.title: Remove the Device From Service
steps.instructions: Once the patient is safely disconnected and treatment is ended, remove the PrismaFlex from clinical use, label it Out of Service, and route it for Clinical Engineering bench evaluation.

steps.title: Service-Level Evaluation
steps.instructions: Verify the specific scale identified by the alarm, perform scale calibration according to approved service procedure, and confirm operation in Diagnose Screen – Scale Diagnose.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Scale Requires Replacement
steps.instructions: If the identified scale remains out of specification after calibration and diagnostic verification, suspect a failed or unstable scale assembly. Replace the scale if indicated and retest the unit.

steps.title: Return to Service Only After Verification
steps.instructions: Confirm the Scales alarm is no longer active, the affected scale passes calibration, Scale Diagnose shows acceptable operation, no related alarms recur during functional testing, and the device is clean and ready for use.

CCR.Complaint: Baxter PrismaFlex reported Alarm: Scales during CRRT setup or treatment. Alarm screen identified an affected scale as out of calibration and the alarm did not clear after RETEST.
CCR.Cause: External checks were performed for bag placement, tubing tension, scale obstruction, visible damage, and fluid contamination. Affected scale remained out of calibration, indicating a scale calibration issue or scale assembly failure.
CCR.Resolution: Device was removed from service after treatment was ended through the appropriate DISCONNECT process. Identified scale was calibrated and verified in Diagnose Screen – Scale Diagnose. Scale was replaced when calibration or diagnostic verification failed.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: Scales Circuit Board
description: Troubleshooting the Scales Circuit Board alarm when PrismaFlex detects a scale hardware failure that may affect CRRT fluid balance monitoring.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Treat the Scales Circuit Board alarm as a serious fluid-management issue. Clinical staff should safely end treatment through DISCONNECT if treatment cannot continue safely. Do not bypass the alarm or continue therapy using unreliable scale data.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact alarm displayed is Scales Circuit Board. Document when it appears, whether it occurs during startup, self-test, treatment, or after movement, and whether other scale-related alarms occurred first.

steps.title: Check External and Simple Causes First
steps.instructions: Inspect the PrismaFlex externally. Verify all scale hooks and bag supports move freely and confirm no bag, tubing, clamp, power cord, or accessory is pulling against the scale assemblies.

steps.title: Verify Proper Bag and Scale Setup
steps.instructions: Confirm bags are hanging on the correct scales and are not resting on the cart, floor, neighboring bags, or tubing. Check for overload, incorrect placement, tubing tension, swinging bags, and damaged or bent hooks.

steps.title: Inspect for Physical Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: With the device removed from treatment use, inspect the scale area for fluid intrusion, cracked housings, loose hooks, impact damage, dried residue, unusual noise, heat, or smell.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only if Clinically Appropriate
steps.instructions: After treatment has been safely ended and the device is not connected to the patient, power the unit down and restart it according to facility practice. Do not power cycle during active therapy unless directed by clinical procedure.

steps.title: Check Scale Operation in Diagnostics
steps.instructions: Use Diagnose Screen – Scale Diagnose to check scale operation. Look for abnormal, frozen, missing, unstable, or clearly incorrect readings and document which scale appears abnormal, if known.

steps.title: Isolate Whether the Problem Is External or Device-Related
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue is related to incorrect bag loading, scale obstruction, external damage, fluid contamination, scale hardware malfunction, or scales circuit board failure.

steps.title: Stop When Resolved
steps.instructions: If an external correction resolves the alarm, run applicable operational checks, verify scale readings respond normally, confirm no scale alarms return, document the correction, and return the unit to service only after functional verification.

CCR.Complaint: PrismaFlex reported Scales Circuit Board alarm during CRRT operation. Alarm indicated hardware failure on the scales circuit board and treatment was ended through DISCONNECT per procedure.
CCR.Cause: External bag loading, scale obstruction, tubing tension, and physical damage were checked. Alarm persisted during evaluation, indicating likely internal scale hardware or scales circuit board failure.
CCR.Resolution: Removed device from service and labeled Out of Service. Checked scale operation in Diagnose Screen – Scale Diagnose. Unit referred for bench repair and affected scale hardware replacement if confirmed.

title: Baxter PrismaFlex Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT) - Alarm: Scale Zero Test
description: Troubleshooting guide for a Baxter PrismaFlex Scale Zero Test alarm caused by scale obstruction, missing carrying bar, bag placement, physical interference, calibration issue, or failed scale zero detection.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the PrismaFlex is connected to an active patient and the alarm affects safe operation, follow clinical protocol and make sure the patient is supported before troubleshooting. Do not ignore a scale-related alarm during CRRT setup or treatment preparation. Do not continue therapy preparation if scale accuracy cannot be trusted.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm and Behavior
steps.instructions: Confirm the displayed alarm is Scale Zero Test. Identify whether the alarm occurs during startup, setup, prime, scale zero testing, or after bags or carrying bars were installed. Observe whether one scale or multiple scales appear affected.

steps.title: Remove Bags From the Scale
steps.instructions: Check each scale and confirm no bag is unexpectedly present during the zero test. Remove any dialysate, replacement, effluent, or accessory bags from scales that should be empty for the zero test. Close the scale and press RETEST.

steps.title: Check for Missing Carrying Bars
steps.instructions: Inspect the carrying bars for each scale location. Confirm all required carrying bars are installed correctly and seated properly. Reinstall any missing or incorrectly positioned carrying bar, then close the scale and press RETEST.

steps.title: Check for Foreign Objects or Physical Interference
steps.instructions: Inspect around each scale and carrying bar for anything touching, hanging, resting, or pulling on the scale. Remove tubing, straps, hooks, debris, cables, or other objects that could affect scale zeroing. Close the scale and press RETEST.

steps.title: Confirm Proper Scale Closure and Machine Position
steps.instructions: Verify that the scale door or scale area is fully closed. Confirm the PrismaFlex is stable and not tilted, bumped, or positioned where tubing or bags are pulling sideways on the scale.

steps.title: Repeat the Zero Test
steps.instructions: After removing bags, reinstalling carrying bars, and clearing interference, close the scale and press RETEST. If the alarm clears, continue setup according to clinical workflow and verify no additional scale alarms return.

CCR.Complaint: Baxter PrismaFlex reported Scale Zero Test alarm during setup. Zero test failed and machine would not proceed normally.
CCR.Cause: Found external scale interference from tubing contacting the carrying bar. Scale could not complete zero test until obstruction was removed.
CCR.Resolution: Removed tubing from scale area, verified carrying bars installed correctly, closed scale, pressed RETEST, and confirmed Scale Zero Test alarm cleared. Device returned to service after successful setup verification.
